Efficient: Not quantified relative to alternatives; no wall-clock, memory, or per-query latency comparison against RL(text)/RL(image) baselines or against full fine-tuning is given. Effective: Partially. Accuracy and VHR gains are statistically resolved, but the same evaluation reveals CORAL is worse than the base on IS_pred (an image-sensitivity proxy), so the paper does not show the model actually became more visually reliant in a global sense, only that it became less prone to one specific asymmetric failure mode. Scalable: Not tested. The evaluation is capped at n=100/benchmark for the primary claims and n=500/benchmark for cross-domain diagnostics; the authors explicitly defer larger-n confirmation of VRS/BD to future camera-ready work rather than presenting it here. Strengths: - The metric suite decomposes a single accuracy number into a family of falsifiable behavioral quantities (VRS, BD, VHR, IS_pred/raw, VBR, HN-GAP), each with a stated failure interpretation, which is more diagnostic than the single blindfolding metric in prior general-domain work. - The paper reports a result unfavorable to its own model (CORAL's higher IS_pred) rather than omitting or downplaying it, and explicitly separates the raw-string IS variant (which would have favored CORAL) from the extracted-answer variant it argues is correct. - The RL(image) text-prior signature (highest no-image accuracy of any model, including the base) is a genuinely informative negative result: it demonstrates that an RL objective trained on image-question-answer triples can still degrade grounding, which is a useful caution against assuming multimodal RL training data implies grounded learning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - Explicitly acknowledged: PMC-VQA train/eval overlap, small n=100 per-benchmark sample size limiting VRS/BD to directional (not resolved) status, IS_pred's inability to distinguish true answer invariance from distinct reasoning paths converging on the same choice, and narrow model scope (Qwen2.5-VL/MedVLThinker family only, no LLaVA-Med/BiomedGPT/HuatuoGPT-Vision). - The authors confront these directly in a dedicated Limitations section rather than burying them, a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No lambda sensitivity analysis is shown despite lambda=0.5 being a load-bearing hyperparameter selected via an unreported validation sweep. - No comparison of CGO against a simpler baseline that penalizes answer changes via synthetic perturbation (blank/shuffle) rather than CLIP-retrieved hard negatives, so the paper does not isolate whether CLIP-based retrieval specifically (versus contrastive training in general, already explored by Liang et al. 2020) drives the gains. - The PathVQA result (accuracy regression, VHR improvement) is interpreted as "the objective suppressing some correct image-invariant predictions along with incorrect ones", a plausible but unverified mechanism; no analysis isolates which specific items regressed or whether they share identifiable characteristics. - No training-run variance (multiple seeds of CORAL fine-tuning) is reported, so it is unclear whether the +6.7 pp accuracy gain would replicate across independent training runs versus reflecting one favorable optimization trajectory. Bottom Line: A practitioner auditing or building medical VLMs gains a reusable, well-instrumented counterfactual evaluation protocol and a sobering empirical finding that RL fine-tuning on image-grounded data does not guarantee grounding, but should treat the CORAL accuracy gains cautiously given the acknowledged PMC-VQA train/eval overlap, the small n=100 per-benchmark sample, and CORAL's own worse-than-baseline IS_pred score.
Search Results 2607.03647 PDF
#17 — Innovative (67)

PixWorld: Unifying 3D Scene Generation and Reconstruction in Pixel Space (46 ▲ )

Sensen Gao, Zhaoqing Wang, Qihang Cao, Dongdong Yu, Changhu Wang, Jia-Wang Bian · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PixWorld, a single 1.04B-parameter diffusion transformer that performs both 3D scene reconstruction and generation by predicting pixel-aligned 3D Gaussians and supervising the diffusion objective directly on rendered multi-view images rather than on VAE/RAE latents. - Why it's important: Prior unified reconstruction-and-generation models (e.g., Gen3R) define their training loss in a pretrained autoencoder's latent space, which introduces information loss and an extra pretraining cost, and decouples the loss from the 3D fidelity it is meant to optimize. - How it does it: PixWorld partitions multi-view input images into a clean subset (driving reconstruction) and a noisy subset (to be generated), decodes both through shared transformer blocks into per-pixel depth and 3D Gaussian attributes, and adds a geometry perception loss that matches rendered and ground-truth views in the feature space of a frozen 3D foundation model (pi-cubed). - The good: On RealEstate10K/DL3DV-10K single-image generation, PixWorld raises PSNR by +1.06 dB and +0.75 dB over the next-best baseline (Gen3R) while lifting AUC@5 pose accuracy from 0.546 to 0.614, a 12.5% relative improvement directly attributable (via ablation) to the geometry perception loss. - The missing: All experiments are confined to indoor/outdoor real-estate-style scene datasets (RealEstate10K, DL3DV-10K, WorldScore) at 336x448 training resolution; there is no evaluation on object-centric scenes, higher resolutions, or scenes with more extreme view counts, so generalization beyond this narrow domain is unestablished. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on feed-forward 3D generation/reconstruction unification or pixel-space diffusion, since it is one of the first papers to demonstrate that ditching the VAE/RAE bottleneck is viable at this scale; less relevant to practitioners needing production-ready inference speed (15s/scene, similar order of magnitude to undistilled competitors, but 1.5x slower than the distilled FlashWorld's 10s).
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (65) and Novelty (70) are the load-bearing dimensions. The paper carries a real controlled ablation (removing the geometry perception loss costs 1.13 dB PSNR and 0.080 AUC@5), uses three standard third-party benchmarks (RealEstate10K, DL3DV-10K, WorldScore), and compares against current baselines (Gen3R, FlashWorld, DepthSplat, YoNoSplat) — a sharp contrast to the qualitative-only evidence in the rest of this cluster. Rigor is capped short of 75 by single-run point estimates with no seeds or error bars, an ablation run at ~15% of the full training budget, and no code or weights release. Novelty (70) reflects a genuine formulation change: moving diffusion supervision into pixel space via differentiable Gaussian rendering, removing the VAE/RAE bottleneck that the directly-competing Gen3R depends on. Non-Obviousness (65) is moderate — 'drop the autoencoder and supervise on renders' is a natural hypothesis, but the geometry-perception critic using a frozen 3D foundation model is a less predictable addition. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) are solid, with honest scope limits in Appendix F.1, though the paper does not discuss why it trails on Content Alignment (55.27 vs. LucidDreamer's 75.00) — a dog-not-barking omission. Practical Impact (45) is low: no artifact released, 15s/scene inference, distillation deferred to future work. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*45 = 67.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: 3D scene reconstruction (recovering geometry from real captures) and 3D scene generation (synthesizing plausible scenes from limited or imagined conditions) have historically been separate research lines: reconstruction uses feed-forward pixel regression, generation uses latent diffusion. Recent attempts to unify them (e.g., Gen3R) still operate in the compressed latent space of a pretrained VAE or Representation Autoencoder (RAE). This creates two concrete problems for anyone building a single model for both tasks: (1) the diffusion objective is defined on intermediate latent features, so the actual 3D output being generated is never directly optimized, and (2) both branches inherit information loss from the autoencoder's compression, which is especially damaging for reconstruction, a fidelity-bound task where any information discarded by the autoencoder cannot be recovered downstream. The autoencoder itself also requires a separate, additional pretraining stage. Key Idea: Move the entire diffusion process into pixel space and define the training objective directly on differentiably rendered images of the predicted 3D Gaussian representation, instead of on encoded latents. Because reconstruction and generation are unified by simply changing which subset of input views is clean (observed) versus noisy (to be denoised), a single forward pass and a single loss formulation cover both tasks without any autoencoder in the loop. Methodology: Given N posed multi-view images, PixWorld partitions view indices into a clean subset Omega_c and a noisy subset Omega_n (Omega_n empty reduces the task to pure reconstruction). A two-stream MMDiT-style transformer (24 layers, hidden width 1024, 16 heads) processes clean and noisy views through separate patchify/projection/adaLN-Zero streams but with joint full attention over the concatenated token axis, sharing a single cross-attention to text tokens and one timestep embedder (noise stream conditioned on sampled t; clean stream fixed at t=1). Each stream's output projects to per-pixel depth and 3D Gaussian attribute heads; Gaussian centers are obtained by unprojecting each pixel using its predicted depth and known camera pose (not directly regressed), and the aggregated per-view Gaussians are merged into one scene-level 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) representation, rendered via a differentiable renderer. Three loss terms compose the objective: (1) L_render, a flow-matching-style loss with an MSE reconstruction term on clean views, a flow-matching velocity term plus LPIPS on noisy/novel views (LPIPS gated to activate only when timestep t exceeds a threshold t_th=0.3, since perceptual supervision is unreliable near pure noise); (2) L_depth, a Huber loss between predicted depth and pseudo-depth labels from DA3; (3) L_geo, the geometry perception loss, a cosine-distance loss in the feature space of a frozen pretrained 3D foundation model (pi-cubed, gradients stopped on the ground-truth branch) that penalizes cross-view 3D geometric inconsistency invisible to 2D photometric/perceptual losses. Design decisions: (a) unprojecting pixels via predicted depth rather than directly regressing 3D centers ties geometry prediction to an interpretable, camera-consistent quantity, well-justified given the ablation showing large PSNR/AUC degradation without geometric supervision; (b) gating LPIPS and L_geo by timestep is a reasonable but empirically-tuned (t_th=0.3) choice with no sensitivity analysis reported; (c) the clean/noisy partition (biased toward small |Omega_c| during training) is an implicit curriculum choice, not derived from first principles. Key Results: On 4-view/8-view reconstruction (RealEstate10K, DL3DV-10K), PixWorld attains the best PSNR and LPIPS across all settings versus MVSplat, DepthSplat, AnySplat, and YoNoSplat (with-pose mode), e.g., 26.21 dB PSNR / 0.138 LPIPS vs. YoNoSplat's 25.86 dB / 0.143 LPIPS at 4-view RealEstate10K. On single-image generation, PixWorld beats LVSM, GF, Gen3C, FlashWorld, and Gen3R, improving PSNR by +1.06 dB on RealEstate10K (18.88 vs. 17.82 for Gen3R) and +0.75 dB on DL3DV-10K (16.50 vs. 15.75), with the largest gains at strict pose thresholds (AUC@5: 0.546 to 0.614). On the WorldScore benchmark (2000 static scenes), PixWorld achieves the best average (71.04) versus Wan-2.1, WonderJourney, LucidDreamer, and FlashWorld, leading on camera control (91.08), 3D consistency (91.39), and photometric consistency (93.84), though it trails LucidDreamer on content alignment (75.00 vs. an unspecified PixWorld score not in the top-2) and FlashWorld/LucidDreamer on object control. Inference is 15s/scene on an A100 GPU with 100 NFEs, comparable in order of magnitude to Gen3C (791s) and Gen3R (882s) but 1.5x slower than the distilled FlashWorld (10s, 4 NFE). All baselines and datasets (RealEstate10K, DL3DV-10K, WorldScore) are standard and current for this subfield; the ablation on geometry perception loss uses a reduced 10K-sequence subset trained for only 30K steps (versus the full model's ~200K steps), a controlled but smaller-scale test. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD and NEW FORMULATION: pixel-space diffusion supervision (adapting the JiT/pixel-diffusion line of work) applied to unify 3D generation and reconstruction via differentiable rendering of a Gaussian representation, eliminating the VAE/RAE bottleneck used by the directly competing Gen3R. The geometry perception loss (matching rendered/GT features in a frozen 3D foundation model's space) is a genuinely new auxiliary supervision signal for this setting, though it reuses an existing pretrained model (pi-cubed) as a frozen critic, a pattern with precedent in perceptual-loss literature (e.g., LPIPS) applied here to 3D geometric features rather than 2D appearance. Rigor & Reproducibility: One ablation is reported (Table 5, Fig. 5): removing the geometry perception loss drops PSNR by 1.13 dB (19.12 to 17.99), SSIM by 0.105, and AUC@5 by 0.080 (~12.5% relative) on a 10K-sequence subset, isolating this component's contribution convincingly. However, no ablations are reported for the depth loss, the LPIPS gating threshold t_th, the clean/noisy partition sampling strategy, or the choice of frozen foundation model (VGGT vs. pi-cubed, both mentioned as options in Sec. 3.3 but only pi-cubed used in experiments). No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere; all numbers appear to be single-run point estimates over 200-scene test sets. No code, data, or model weights release is mentioned in the paper body. Evaluation uses standard third-party benchmarks (RealEstate10K, DL3DV-10K, WorldScore) with baseline numbers apparently reproduced by the authors (WorldScore baseline numbers explicitly stated as taken from the official release), which is a reasonable practice but leaves open whether the authors' own reproductions of LVSM/GF/Gen3C/FlashWorld/Gen3R match those methods' original reported numbers. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (feed-forward model, evaluated by experiments across four protocols). Practical: yes, trained and evaluated on standard NVIDIA A800/A100 GPUs (32 GPUs for ~200K training steps; single GPU for inference), consistent with academic-lab-scale compute. Efficient: inference takes 15s/scene at 336x448 resolution with 100 NFE, in the same order of magnitude as Gen3C/Gen3R (both >750s more expensive per scene by frame count, but their absolute wall-clock is higher due to denoising long video sequences of 24-121 frames vs. PixWorld's 8 keyframes) and slower than the distilled FlashWorld (10s, 4 NFE); the paper explicitly frames distillation as future work rather than an achieved efficiency gain. Effective: reconstruction PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS and generation NVS/pose metrics directly measure the stated 3D fidelity and consistency goals rather than a loose proxy, and the geometry-perception ablation demonstrates causal contribution to 3D structural accuracy, not just 2D appearance quality. Scalable: the paper explicitly lists scaling to higher resolution and more diverse (outdoor, object-centric) scenes as open limitations (Appendix F.1); no experiment tests behavior at higher resolution, larger view counts, or on datasets beyond RealEstate10K/DL3DV-10K/WorldScore, so scalability claims beyond the tested regime are unverified. Delivers a trained model architecture and training recipe as described in the paper; no software artifact (code/weights/dataset) release is stated. Strengths: - Demonstrates, for the first time at this scale (1.04B parameters, ~200K training steps, 67K scenes), that pixel-space diffusion with differentiable rendering can match state-of-the-art feed-forward reconstruction methods (DepthSplat, YoNoSplat) while also outperforming latent-space unified generation-and-reconstruction methods (Gen3R), a genuine empirical validation of removing the autoencoder bottleneck rather than just a theoretical argument. - The geometry perception loss is a specific, well-motivated mechanism (using a frozen 3D foundation model's cross-view-aware feature space as a structural critic) that the ablation shows meaningfully improves both novel-view fidelity and camera-pose control accuracy, addressing a real failure mode (depth drift, floater averaging) that 2D photometric losses cannot detect. - Careful engineering of the flow-matching objective to handle the mixed clean/noisy view setting, including gating perceptual and geometric losses by timestep to avoid unreliable supervision near pure noise, and using depth-based unprojection rather than direct 3D center regression to keep Gaussian centers camera-consistent. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state in Appendix F.1 that experiments are confined to widely used scene-level datasets (RealEstate10K, DL3DV-10K) and that further evaluation on more diverse outdoor and object-centric scenes is needed to characterize generalization. They also acknowledge training under finite resolution (336x448) and compute budgets, leaving room for higher-resolution fidelity improvements, and note that inference speed (15s/scene) remains a target for future distillation and quantization work. These are direct, specific limitations rather than vague disclaimers, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The paper does not report per-parameter or FLOPs comparisons against baselines, only wall-clock time; since PixWorld (1.04B params) is compared against baselines of unstated parameter counts, it is unclear whether its gains come from architectural advantages or simply from being a larger, more heavily trained model (200K steps, 32 A800 GPUs, plus 10M auxiliary BLIP-3o images) than some competitors. - The ablation study (Table 5) uses a 10K-sequence subset trained for only 30K steps, roughly 15% of the full model's training budget; whether the geometry perception loss's ~12.5% relative AUC@5 gain persists at full training scale is not verified, only assumed. - WorldScore Table 4 shows PixWorld trailing on Object Control (46.25 vs. LucidDreamer's 41.18 highlighted as second-best, though the paper's own bolding pattern is inconsistent here) and Content Alignment (55.27 vs. LucidDreamer's 75.00), a dog-not-barking omission since the text emphasizes PixWorld's best average and three category wins without discussing why it underperforms on text-prompt-following metrics specifically. - No sensitivity analysis is given for the timestep gating threshold t_th=0.3, a hyperparameter that directly controls when the paper's two most novel loss terms (LPIPS, geometry perception) are active; given its stated importance to avoiding "unreliable" supervision near pure noise, the specific value's robustness is unestablished. Bottom Line: A practitioner or researcher building feed-forward 3D generation-and-reconstruction systems gains a concrete, empirically validated argument (backed by a real ablation) that pixel-space diffusion with a geometry-aware auxiliary loss outperforms the latent-space unification approach (Gen3R) it directly targets, though the narrow dataset scope (indoor/outdoor real-estate scenes only) and lack of released code/weights mean the generalization claim rests on trust in the reported numbers rather than independent reproduction.
Recommended 2607.05373 PDF
#18 — Innovative (67)

Poisson-Gamma Modeling of Inter-Relational Dependencies in Dynamic Knowledge Graphs

N Fang, Y Wang, H Liao, S Yang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (70) and Novelty (68) carry the score: the PGE-vs-PGRE ablation directly isolates the relation-transition matrix rather than asserting its value, results are 5-seed with standard deviations, code is public, and low effective sample sizes (12.99-49.97/1000) are disclosed rather than buried. Non-Obviousness (70) credits the specific insight — embedding a Dirichlet relation-transition kernel inside the Gamma-Markov recursion so one relation's community activity mixes over all relations' prior state — which is a targeted mechanism, not a recombination for its own sake, though the surrounding Bernoulli-Poisson/CRT machinery is reused wholesale from Zhou 2015 and Schein et al. Completeness (70) is helped by plainly stated future-work limitations (fixed Pi, fixed node set) but hurt by a fixed K=200 with no sensitivity sweep despite the shrinkage claim resting on it. Resilience (65) is the weak dimension: all benchmarks are pruned to 500 entities and 8-15 relations, only a single test time step is evaluated, and the LLM-based temporal KG forecasters the paper's own Related Work cites (Bai 2025, Tang 2025) are excluded from comparison, so the 40-100% AUC-PR gains are untested against the acknowledged frontier. Impact (55) reflects released code offset by 9-100+ hour Gibbs runs and no evidence of tractability past toy scale. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*55 = 67.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full summary & analysis
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PGRE (Poisson-Gamma Relational Evolution), a Bayesian generative model for dynamic knowledge graphs that couples multi-relational link formation to a Gamma-Markov community-weight process governed by a learned relation-transition matrix. - Why it's important: Dynamic knowledge graph completion in sparse, small-sample regimes (common in real deployments) is where neural black-box models lose their advantage, and interpretable Bayesian alternatives that explicitly model relation-to-relation dependence are underexplored. - How it does it: It represents each relation-community weight at time t as a Gamma draw whose rate depends on a Dirichlet-distributed relation-transition matrix Pi applied to all relations' weights at t-1, embedding cross-relation temporal coupling directly into the Gamma-Markov prior, with inference via a negative-binomial/CRT-augmented Gibbs sampler. - The good: On ICEWS18 and GDELT, PGRE roughly doubles to triples AUC-PR over every probabilistic and neural baseline tested (e.g., 0.198 vs. 0.127 best baseline on ICEWS18; 0.277 vs. 0.136 best baseline on GDELT), with the ablation (PGE, no transition matrix) showing a substantial AUC-PR drop, directly attributing the gain to the transition mechanism. - The missing: All three benchmarks are pruned to the 500 most active entities and 8-15 most frequent relations before evaluation, so there is no evidence the Poisson-multinomial allocation and O(TRK) Gibbs updates remain tractable at realistic entity/relation counts (thousands to millions), and runtime is reported only as seconds/iteration with no total wall-clock or iteration-count-to-convergence comparison. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on Bayesian dynamic network models or sparse-regime KG completion; skip if the interest is state-of-the-art large-scale KG forecasting, where the entity/relation subsampling limits direct relevance. Problem & Motivation: Dynamic knowledge graphs (molecular, social, textual) evolve over time and are frequently sparse or small-sample, a regime where neural temporal KG embedding methods (Know-Evolve, RE-NET, RE-GCN, DyRep) tend to overfit or lose interpretability. Prior Bayesian dynamic-network models (stochastic block models, IRM, GP-EPM, D-NGPPF, DPGM, G-HSEPM) either target single, undirected networks or, when extended to multi-relational count tensors (PGDS, PRGDS, NBRGDS), fold relation structure into a data reshaping step rather than modeling relation-to-relation dependence directly. The gap addressed: no existing Bayesian model explicitly represents how one relation type's occurrence at time t depends on another relation type's state at t-1 (e.g., "collaborates with" raising the likelihood of a later "publishes with" link) while retaining closed-form posterior inference. Key Idea: A relation-transition matrix Pi_rr' is embedded inside the Gamma-Markov recursion for the community weights delta_k^(t,r), i.e., delta_k^(t,r) ~ Gamma(sum_r' Pi_rr' delta_k^(t-1,r'), 1/tau), so the temporal evolution of a given relation's latent community activity is itself a weighted mixture across all relations' prior-step activity. This is the single mechanism distinguishing PGRE from a same-model ablation (PGE) that drops Pi and evolves each relation's weights independently. Methodology: The generative pipeline has three layers. (1) Entity layer: each entity gets Gamma-distributed subject-side (phi_ik) and object-side (psi_jk) community-factor loadings over K latent communities. (2) Link layer: a Bernoulli-Poisson link, M_ij^(t,r) = 1(x_ij^(t,r) >= 1) with x_ij^(t,r) ~ Poisson(sum_k delta_k^(t,r) phi_ik psi_jk), ties community weights and factor loadings to observed binary adjacency. (3) Temporal-relational layer: delta_k^(t,r) evolves via the Pi-weighted Gamma-Markov chain described above, with Pi_r ~ Dirichlet(nu_1 nu_r,...,xi nu_r,...,nu_R nu_r) inducing self-persistence (via xi) plus cross-relation transition mass, and nu_r ~ Gamma(gamma_0/R, 1/beta) providing a hierarchical shrinkage prior that lets K be set large (K=200 in experiments) while redundant communities shrink toward zero. Inference is a Gibbs sampler exploiting Poisson-multinomial and negative-binomial/CRT augmentation (Theorems 1-3, standard results from Zhou 2015, not new here) to obtain closed-form Gamma/Dirichlet/Beta conditional posteriors for every parameter, avoiding variational approximation or gradient-based training entirely. Three design decisions stand out: (a) using a shared Dirichlet-distributed Pi rather than a full R x R free parameter matrix constrains the transition kernel to be row-stochastic, which is justified for identifiability but caps expressiveness of the transition structure; (b) the hierarchical Gamma prior on nu_r for automatic community-count shrinkage is a reused Zhou 2015 technique, not new to this paper; (c) fixing K=200 for all datasets/methods in experiments (per the supplementary) means the reported shrinkage property is asserted, not directly demonstrated via a K-sensitivity sweep in the main results. Key Results: Evaluated on ICEWS18, GDELT, and WIKI (all subsampled to 500 entities and the 8-15 most frequent relation types), measured by AUC-PR and AUC-ROC, averaged over 5 seeds with reported standard deviations. PGRE achieves best AUC-PR on ICEWS18 (0.198 vs. best baseline D-NGPPF 0.127) and GDELT (0.277 vs. best baseline PRGDS 0.136), roughly 40-100% relative improvement, while AUC-ROC is close to but not always the best (e.g., RE-NET's 0.936 on ICEWS18 vs PGRE's own comparable 0.936; RE-NET wins outright on WIKI with AUC-PR 0.727 vs PGRE's 0.607). Baselines span both probabilistic (PGDS, PRGDS, NBRGDS, D-NGPPF, DPGM, G-HSEPM) and neural (Know-Evolve, DyRep, RE-NET) families and are reasonably current (RE-NET is 2019, G-HSEPM is 2025), though none are LLM-based temporal KG forecasters mentioned in Related Work (e.g., Bai et al. 2025, Tang et al. 2025), which are cited as recent but excluded from comparison. Runtime: PGRE has the lowest per-iteration cost on ICEWS18 (71.70s) and WIKI (16.19s) among all 7 baselines, second-lowest on GDELT. Ablation across relation counts R in {10,25,50,75,100} on ICEWS18 shows PGRE's AUC-PR peaking at R=25 and staying above D-NGPPF and RE-NET throughout, while competitors' AUC-PR degrades as R grows, supporting the claim that the transition mechanism specifically helps as relational diversity increases. Scale tested is small (500 entities, <=15 relations, single test time step T) — standard KG benchmark subsets, not stress scale. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (the Pi-weighted Gamma-Markov coupling across relations) applied within an established NEW FORMULATION lineage (Bernoulli-Poisson link, hierarchical Gamma-Dirichlet priors, CRT/negative-binomial augmentation are all reused directly from Zhou 2015, Schein et al. 2016/2019, and Yang & Koeppl 2018a/b). The genuinely novel element is narrow: inserting an explicit relation-transition kernel into an otherwise standard Poisson-Gamma dynamical system for multi-relational (rather than single-relation) temporal networks. The rest is a competent recombination of known Bayesian nonparametric machinery applied to a new setting (multi-relational DKGs). Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation (PGE vs. PGRE) isolates the contribution of the transition matrix and shows a clear AUC-PR drop when removed, which is the paper's strongest rigor evidence. Results are averaged over 5 seeds with reported standard deviations for the main table, which is good practice; however, the R-sensitivity ablation (Fig. 3) and the temporal-scale sensitivity analysis (Table 4, supplementary) are described as "single-run," so those secondary results have no variance estimate. Code and scripts are publicly released (https://github.com/ffffkgh/PGRE, per supplementary). Datasets and preprocessing (top-500 entities, top-8/10/15 relations) are the same subsampling protocol across all methods, which is fair for comparison but narrows what can be concluded about full-scale KG performance. Effective sample sizes for select transition parameters are as low as 12.99-49.97 out of 1000 retained post-burn-in samples (Table 3, supplementary), indicating meaningfully autocorrelated chains for at least some latent community-weight summaries; the paper discloses this directly rather than hiding it. Deployability (4-property check): Profile = EMPIRICAL (Bayesian model evaluated via link-prediction experiments). - Practical: Runs on a single workstation-class GPU/CPU setup (Dell Precision 7920, per supplementary); Gibbs sampling with 2000 iterations completed at 16-186 seconds per iteration across datasets, translating to roughly 9-100+ hours total, which is heavy relative to a forward pass of a trained neural model but plausible for offline analysis. - Efficient: Lowest or near-lowest per-iteration cost among 7 baselines on all 3 datasets (Table 2), but this is against other MCMC/probabilistic and neural methods of comparable complexity, not against lightweight heuristics; the O(TRK) per-relation Gamma updates plus the CRT/multinomial augmentation add non-trivial constant-factor overhead per iteration versus a plain forward-only neural model. - Effective: AUC-PR gains on ICEWS18/GDELT are measured directly (not a proxy) on the stated task (relation prediction at a held-out query time), and the ablation supports a causal link between the transition kernel and the improvement, though AUC-ROC gains are marginal and WIKI is a loss to RE-NET. - Scalable: Not tested. Entity count is fixed at 500 and relation count at <=15 in the main experiments; the R-ablation only reaches R=100 on ICEWS18. No experiment establishes behavior at 5,000+ entities or 50+ relations, and Gibbs sampling's typical scaling weaknesses (per-iteration cost growing with N^2 R K for pairwise counts) are not discussed. Strengths: - The explicit relation-transition matrix inside the Gamma-Markov recursion is a targeted, well-motivated mechanism (Fig. 1's intuition of relation-to-relation state transitions) with an ablation that directly isolates its contribution rather than asserting it. - Sparse/small-sample robustness is demonstrated empirically, not just claimed: PGRE's AUC-PR advantage over both probabilistic and neural baselines is largest on the sparsest, most multi-relational settings (ICEWS18, GDELT), which is the paper's stated target regime. - Interpretability is substantiated with concrete artifacts: learned transition-probability heatmaps (Fig. 5) showing a dominant diagonal (self-persistence) plus specific off-diagonal bands, and per-relation latent-factor activity trajectories (Fig. 4), not just a qualitative claim of interpretability. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state in Future Work that the model does not yet handle rapidly evolving event streams (implying a discrete time-step limitation) or time-varying transition dynamics (Pi is fixed across all time steps within a run), and note the framework currently assumes a shared, fixed node set across time rather than heterogeneous/partially-overlapping entities. These are stated plainly as directions for future work rather than buried. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No comparison against the LLM-based temporal KG forecasting methods the Related Work section itself cites as recent (Bai et al. 2025, Tang et al. 2025), leaving the strength of the AUC-PR gains untested against the current frontier the paper acknowledges exists. - The 500-entity / 8-15-relation subsampling is applied uniformly, but the paper does not report how much of the original graph's structure (entities, edges) this discards, so the reader cannot judge how representative the evaluated subgraphs are of the full ICEWS18/GDELT/WIKI datasets. - K=200 is fixed across all datasets and never varied in the main paper despite the paper's own claim that the hierarchical Gamma prior lets K be set generously and self-prune; no sensitivity analysis over K is shown (only tau is swept, in the supplementary), so the shrinkage claim rests on architecture, not demonstrated empirical robustness to K choice. - Only a single test time step (t_q = T) is evaluated per run; the paper does not report performance over a multi-step forecasting horizon, so claims about "tracking relational evolution over time" are supported by static single-point-in-time prediction accuracy rather than a rolling forecast evaluation. Bottom Line: A practitioner building interpretable Bayesian models for sparse multi-relational dynamic graphs gains a concrete, ablation-validated mechanism (relation-coupled Gamma-Markov weights) worth adopting, but anyone needing large-scale or multi-horizon temporal KG forecasting should look elsewhere, since PGRE's evaluation stops at 500-entity subgraphs and single-step prediction.
Search Results 2607.02872 PDF
#19 — Innovative (66)

UniClawBench: A Universal Benchmark for Proactive Agents on Real-World Tasks (22 ▲ )

Zhekai Chen, Chengqi Duan, Kaiyue Sun, Bohao Li, Yuqing Wang, Manyuan Zhang, Xihui Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces UniClawBench, a 400-task bilingual benchmark that evaluates proactive LLM agents in live Docker environments across five capability dimensions (Skill Usage, Exploration, Long-Context Reasoning, Multimodal Understanding, Cross-Platform Coordination), using a three-role closed-loop evaluation scheme (executor, hidden supervisor, user simulator) instead of single-turn static-answer grading. - Why it's important: Existing agent benchmarks rely on sandboxed mirrors, single-turn grading, and scenario-based task labels that conflate distinct failure causes, so practitioners cannot tell whether a failure stems from weak tool use, poor visual grounding, or bad long-horizon memory. - How it does it: Checkpoint-based rubrics replace fixed ground-truth answers (tolerating a dynamic real world), and an information-firewalled three-agent loop (hidden supervisor scores against private rubrics, a separate user-simulator gives only sanitized natural-language feedback) enables multi-turn correction without leaking grading criteria to the executor. - The good: A 50-trajectory human-expert reliability study shows 92.0% pass/fail agreement with majority-vote human judgment and Pearson r = 0.71 / Spearman rho = 0.68 correlation between the checkpoint-based score and human completion scores, giving the automated grading empirical credibility. - The missing: The benchmark has no adversarial or held-out check on whether the hidden supervisor and user-simulator (both GPT-5.4-based Codex agents) themselves introduce systematic bias or exploitable patterns the tested executor models could learn to game across repeated runs. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or evaluating computer-use/proactive agents (OpenClaw, Nanobot-style systems); the capability-decomposed, closed-loop design is a more diagnostic alternative to scenario-based benchmarks like WebArena or OSWorld.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (70) and Novelty (72) lead: the information-firewall between a rubric-holding hidden supervisor and a rubric-blind user simulator is a non-obvious resolution of a real tension (realistic multi-turn feedback without leaking grading criteria), and checkpoint rubrics over frozen ground truth is a principled answer to live-web mutability. Rigor (62) is pulled down by single-run PR/AS cells across 10 models and 3 frameworks with no repeat-run variance despite stochastic executors and live web dependencies, no ablation isolating which of the three design pieces produces the reported reliability, and a GPT-5.4/Codex supervisor grading GPT-5.4 among the executors with no vendor-bias check. The 92.0% human agreement and r=0.71 correlation on 50 trajectories is a genuine methodological control that most benchmark papers skip. Completeness (70) and Resilience (60) reflect direct limitation statements offset by unstressed firewall claims and manual, un-agreement-checked task-to-capability assignment. Impact (65) reflects a released 400-task suite that discriminates real gaps (all 10 SOTA models below 0.50 PR). Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*65 = 66.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Proactive agents (OpenClaw, Nanobot, Hermes Agent) increasingly operate real browsers, terminals, and desktop apps as always-on digital coworkers, but the paper argues existing agent benchmarks (WebArena, OSWorld, AgentBench, GAIA, tau-bench) fail them along three axes: (1) they run on self-hosted mirrors or cached pages rather than the live, mutable real world, producing a gap between sandbox and real capability; (2) they use single-turn evaluation, ignoring that real users give iterative follow-up feedback; (3) they organize tasks by application scenario ("office", "shopping"), which conflates multiple underlying capabilities so a failed task does not localize to a specific bottleneck (perception, tool use, memory, coordination). The authors target researchers and developers of proactive agent frameworks who need to diagnose root causes of failure, not just an aggregate success rate. Key Idea: The core trick is decoupling what is graded from how much is revealed: checkpoint-based rubrics (rather than frozen ground-truth answers) let a hidden supervisor score dynamically-changing real-world outcomes, while an information firewall between the supervisor (has full grading criteria) and the user simulator (only sees a coarse pass/fail/continue signal plus the visible trajectory) allows realistic multi-turn feedback without leaking the rubric to the agent under test. This resolves what the authors call the "information-isolation paradox" of closed-loop evaluation in live environments. Methodology: Each of 400 manually constructed bilingual (200 English, 200 Chinese) tasks is a self-contained package: task instruction, injected resources/files, declared skills, docker-compose service definitions, and a hidden `eval_rule.md` with a nine-section rubric (grading philosophy, task contract, source-resolution rules, ground-truth snapshot, weighted checkpoint rubric summing to 1.00, scoring caps, pass/continue/fail banding, hidden reference inventory, and static-vs-live content notes). Execution: the executor agent runs inside a fresh Docker container (2GB RAM) under a supported framework (OpenClaw, Nanobot, or EDICT), interacting with browsers, GUI apps, local files, and injected services; after each turn the runner collects trajectory, tool-use records, and saved artifacts. The hidden supervisor (a GPT-5.4-based Codex agent with high reasoning effort) scores against the private rubric and emits pass/fail/continue plus a justification; on a recoverable "continue" state, a separate user-simulator agent (also GPT-5.4/Codex) generates natural follow-up feedback from only the visible trajectory and the coarse status signal, which is further sanitized by a feedback rewriter before being returned to the executor. Loops end at pass, irrecoverable fail, follow-up budget exhaustion (max 2 follow-ups), or timeout (30 min global / 20 min per-turn standard, 45/30 min for long-context tasks). Two headline metrics are reported: Pass Rate (PR, binary) and Average Score (AS, mean checkpoint score); timed-out runs are credited with their best achieved score rather than zero. Three design decisions stand out: (1) capability-oriented rather than scenario-oriented task assignment, justified by the argument that it isolates the bottleneck capability, though assignment of a task to a single "primary" capability is a manual, somewhat subjective judgment call not otherwise validated; (2) checkpoint rubrics instead of fixed answers, well-motivated by the dynamic-web problem (e.g., changing prices) but adds subjectivity that the human-agreement study only partially bounds; (3) the firewall design (supervisor blind to user simulator, user simulator blind to rubric) is a reasonable mitigation for information leakage but is asserted rather than empirically stress-tested (e.g., no experiment shows the executor cannot infer rubric structure from repeated feedback patterns). Key Results: Across 10 SOTA models under the OpenClaw framework (Table 1), overall pass rates stay strictly below 50% for every model; the best are Claude Opus-4.8 (PR 0.475, AS 0.702) and GPT-5.4 (PR 0.407, AS 0.774), with open-source Qwen-3.5-Plus (PR 0.362, AS 0.709) and Kimi-2.6 (PR 0.362, AS 0.709) closely trailing or matching closed models like Gemini-3.1-Pro (PR 0.407, AS 0.774 tie region) -- notably some open-weight models beat some closed ones. Per-capability, models do best on Skill Usage (best PR 0.550, Claude Opus-4.8) and Exploration (best PR 0.775) but drop sharply on Long-Context (best PR 0.438), Multimodal (best PR 0.212), and Cross-Platform (best PR 0.388) tasks. A "halfway failure" pattern recurs: AS is consistently much higher than PR, meaning models make partial, checkpoint-scoring progress but fail to close out tasks reliably. Cross-framework comparison (Table 2, 3 models x 3 frameworks) shows OpenClaw achieves the highest pass rate for every tested model (e.g., Claude Opus-4.8: PR 0.475 under OpenClaw vs. 0.415 under EDICT vs. 0.385 under Nanobot), and the framework gap widens as base-model capability increases -- framework choice measurably outweighs model choice in the paper's own framing. EDICT shows high AS but lower PR ("coordination friction" from multi-agent orchestration); Nanobot uses far fewer tokens (e.g., 0.57M vs. 1.15M average input tokens for GPT-5.4 on OpenClaw) but at a pass-rate cost. A reliability study on 50 trajectories against 3 human experts finds 92.0% PR agreement and Pearson r=0.71/Spearman rho=0.68 for AS. All experiments run on Intel i7-13700 hosts with 2GB per-container memory; average task time is 17.4 minutes. Baselines are the models under test themselves (10 current frontier/open models circa the paper's stated 2026 releases), not prior benchmarks' published numbers, so there is no head-to-head comparison against, e.g., WebArena or OSWorld results on the same models. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET (400 capability-decomposed, bilingual, live-environment tasks) + NEW EVALUATION METHOD (three-role closed-loop supervisor/user-simulator/executor with information firewall and checkpoint rubrics replacing static ground truth). The genuinely new element is the closed-loop, information-isolated multi-turn evaluation protocol for dynamic real-world settings; the capability taxonomy itself is a reorganization of task categorization (a formulation contribution) rather than a new capability concept, and the underlying agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Nanobot, EDICT) and models evaluated are all pre-existing third-party systems. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper includes one dedicated reliability/validity check (human-vs-automatic agreement on 50 trajectories, PR agreement 92%, AS correlation r=0.71), which is a genuine and welcome methodological control. However, there are no ablations isolating which piece of the three-role design (checkpoint rubrics vs. firewall vs. multi-turn loop) drives the reported reliability, no repeated-seed or variance reporting for the main cross-model/cross-framework tables (single run per model/framework cell, as best can be told), and no significance testing on the PR/AS differences between models or frameworks. Both the supervisor and user simulator are built on the same GPT-5.4/Codex family used partly as an executor elsewhere in the paper's own comparisons, raising a same-vendor-grading-same-vendor concern that is not discussed. Code and benchmark are stated as publicly released at a GitHub URL (https://github.com/HKU-MMLab/UniClawBench), which supports reproducibility of the task suite itself, though live/dynamic web-dependent tasks (explicitly acknowledged in the eval-rule's "Dynamic Content Note" section) are inherently less reproducible over time. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (a benchmark paper reporting agent execution results, no primary formal proof and no persistent deployed system beyond the benchmark harness itself). - Practical: Yes; the harness runs on commodity hardware (Intel i7-13700, 16GB RAM, 2GB/container), making the benchmark itself easy to reproduce locally. - Efficient: Token cost is substantial and framework-dependent (up to 1.15M input tokens per task for GPT-5.4 on OpenClaw, and EDICT described as consuming "a huge amount" of tokens), a real practical cost the paper reports but does not optimize. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrates that it discriminates real capability gaps (sub-50% pass rates across all 10 SOTA models, clear degradation on long-context/multimodal/cross-platform tasks) and correlates with human judgment, so it appears to measure genuine task competence rather than a proxy, within the scope of the 400 designed tasks. - Scalable: Not tested; the benchmark is fixed at 400 manually curated tasks, and the paper does not address what happens with 10x-100x more tasks, models, or frameworks, nor the cost of maintaining live-web tasks over time as pages change. The paper delivers a public benchmark suite plus evaluation code (a software/dataset artifact) rather than a reusable proof technique or a deployed production system. Strengths: - The capability-oriented taxonomy with checkpoint rubrics is a concrete, actionable fix to the scenario-conflation problem that afflicts most existing agent benchmarks, and the paper backs the taxonomy with task-composition statistics (Figure 3) showing genuine diversity across application domains, input types, and output types rather than a narrow task set. - The information-firewalled three-role closed-loop design is a non-obvious solution to a real tension (need for realistic multi-turn feedback vs. risk of leaking grading criteria), validated empirically via the 92% human-agreement figure rather than just asserted. - Cross-framework evaluation (OpenClaw vs. Nanobot vs. EDICT) on the same models is a distinct and useful contribution beyond cross-model comparison; it produces the actionable, if unsurprising, finding that framework/orchestration design can dominate base-model capability differences in end-to-end task success. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state three limitations in the conclusion: (1) the benchmark is limited to a relatively small set of 400 manually curated tasks; (2) live-environment execution introduces possible instability (the dynamic real world can change between runs); (3) LLM-based evaluation (supervisor and user simulator) may introduce bias. This is a direct, non-minimizing acknowledgment of exactly the weaknesses a reviewer would raise. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No variance/repeat-run reporting: with live web dependencies and stochastic LLM executors, single-run PR/AS numbers per model/framework cell could shift substantially on re-run; the paper reports point estimates only. - The supervisor and user-simulator are both GPT-5.4/Codex-based, and GPT-5.4 is also one of the executor models being scored elsewhere in the same tables; the paper does not check whether this shared-vendor setup systematically favors or disfavors OpenAI-family executors versus Claude or open-weight executors. - Task-to-capability assignment ("each task is assigned to the category that best captures its primary capability bottleneck") is done manually by the authors with no inter-annotator agreement reported, despite this categorization being the paper's central organizing claim. - No comparison against prior benchmarks (WebArena, OSWorld, GAIA, ClawBench) on the same models to show UniClawBench yields different or more diagnostic conclusions than those existing tools would on the identical model set. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating proactive real-world agents gains a genuinely more diagnostic evaluation methodology here (capability decomposition plus closed-loop, leak-resistant multi-turn grading, validated against human judges), even though the underlying dataset is modest in size and the automated-judge chain (GPT-5.4-based supervisor and user simulator scoring GPT-5.4 among other executors) deserves more scrutiny than the paper gives it.
Recommended 2607.08768 PDF
#20 — Innovative (66)

Multi-Turn Agentic Scientific Literature Search via Workflow Induction (16 ▲ )

Jisen Li, Bingxuan Li, Nanyi Jiang, Xuying Ning, Xiyao Wang, Yifan Shen, Heng Wang, Yuqing Jian, Xiaoxia Wu, Ben Athiwaratkun, Pan Lu, Jiaxuan You, Bingxin Zhao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 7 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PaperPilot, a multi-turn literature-search agent that treats scientific search as workflow induction, constructing and iteratively editing an executable DAG of typed paper-search operators (keyword search, citation expansion, filtering, scoring, reranking, evidence extraction) in response to user feedback. - Why it's important: Fixed retrieval pipelines and free-form language-only reasoning make search strategies opaque and hard to steer, so researchers cannot inspect or correct why an agent retrieved a given paper set when their intent is underspecified or evolves across turns. - How it does it: User feedback is converted into concrete graph edits (add/modify/remove nodes) over an explicit, type-checked DAG rather than being folded back in as more query text, and the 9B model is trained with supervised workflow imitation followed by DPO-style preference optimization over corrupted workflow negatives. - The good: PaperPilot-9B raises the base Qwen3.5-9B toolset agent's Hit@5 from 58.0 to 77.0 and eliminates workflow execution errors (9.5% to 0%) under multi-turn interaction, at $0.0180/case versus $6.09/case for OpenAI DeepResearch. - The missing: All quantitative retrieval results depend on a single LLM-simulated user (Qwen3.5-397B) for multi-turn feedback; the small independent human study (6 volunteers, 5 sessions each) is the only real-user evidence and covers far fewer conditions. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building tool-augmented literature-search or research agents who wants a concrete DAG-based alternative to free-form ReAct-style tool loops; skip if the interest is in the underlying retrieval/reranking algorithms themselves, which are standard components reused off the shelf.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (68) are the strengths: representing the search strategy itself as a typed, executable DAG so user feedback becomes a graph edit rather than appended query text is a real formulation move, and the corrupted-workflow DPO negative construction targets the specific structural failures (invalid references, dropped nodes) that 9B-scale generation actually produces. Rigor (65) is capped by the fact that every headline multi-turn number depends on a single Qwen3.5-397B simulated user, no seeds or variance appear anywhere (so the 58.0 to 77.0 Hit@5 gain cannot be checked against run-to-run noise), and the gold sets are LLM-assisted in a way not detailed enough to rule out circularity with the LLM systems being scored. Resilience (60) reflects that the only real-user evidence is a 6-participant study spanning three unrelated majors for a CS-search task. Completeness (68) credits four honest, specific limitations. Impact (68) is strong on cost-effectiveness ($0.0180/case vs $6.09 for DeepResearch, with released model and toolset) but the best system in the table is still GPT-5.4 at Hit@5=84.0. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*68 = 66.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 68 Resilience 60 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Researchers routinely need more than single-query retrieval: identifying predecessor work, follow-up work, sibling methods, benchmark papers, or surveys, where the correct retrieval strategy (citation direction, recency filtering, methodological similarity) depends on latent, evolving intent that is rarely fully specified upfront. Existing literature-search agents (LitLLM, PaperQA2, OpenScholar, AutoSurvey, ResearchAgent, PaSa, SPAR, Elicit, OpenAI DeepResearch) either execute a fixed retrieval-and-synthesis pipeline or represent user intent implicitly in free-form language reasoning traces, so when a user says "these papers are too broad" the agent has no structured way to translate that into a concrete change in retrieval behavior. Key Idea: Represent the search strategy itself as an explicit, typed, executable DAG (nodes = operator instantiations with parameters, edges = type-checked data flow of paper sets/scores/keywords/evidence), so that user feedback becomes a first-class edit operation over this graph (add-node, modify-node, remove-node) rather than additional free-text appended to the query. This makes the retrieval strategy inspectable and directly editable, and lets a small trained model learn to imitate and refine workflows rather than only refine query strings. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages: (1) Induce — given query, anchor paper, candidate set, and history, the agent selects operators from PaperPilot-Toolset (17 operators across sourcing, combine/filter, scoring, cutting, rerank, LLM-keyword-generation, and output/graph-construction categories, Table 5) and assigns parameters to build a valid DAG; (2) Execute — the DAG runs node-by-node with cached intermediate outputs (paper sets, scores, keywords, evidence); (3) Refine — user feedback triggers Refine(G_t, f_t, H_{t+1}) which edits DAG structure/parameters rather than restarting the pipeline. Training is two-phase: SFT on 5,540 workflow-supervision examples distilled from a strong teacher model across 2,723 anchor-query cases (filtered to require the gold paper in the top-5 with direction-specific success), followed by IPO-style DPO (β=0.2) over 1,733 hard chosen-rejected pairs built by corrupting successful teacher workflows with structural (invalid references, dropped nodes, incorrect operators) and semantic (vague filter/NLI parameters) errors. Two design decisions stand out: opaque per-run node/paper labels are not used here (that's the companion thematic-grouper design elsewhere), but the DAG's strict type-consistency constraint (predecessor output type must match successor input type) is well-justified as a mechanism to keep LLM-generated workflows executable; the choice to derive preference pairs from synthetic corruptions rather than mining real failure cases is more debatable, since it may not cover the failure distribution actually produced by imperfect 9B-scale generation. Key Results: On a held-out benchmark (five search directions: predecessor, successor, sibling, benchmark, survey; 6–15 gold papers per case, Semantic Scholar metadata), PaperPilot-9B (multi-turn, trained) achieves Hit@5=77.0, Hit@10=83.5, Hit@15=89.5, Recall@50=40.0, MRR=59.4, nDCG@10=32.5 at $0.0180/case, versus the untrained Qwen3.5-9B+Toolset baseline's Hit@5=58.0, MRR=47.5, nDCG@10=26.8, error rate 9.5%. GPT-5.4 with Web Search + PaperPilot-Toolset in multi-turn is the overall best system (Hit@5=84.0, MRR=71.8) but at $0.1508/case, roughly 8x PaperPilot-9B's cost; OpenAI DeepResearch, evaluated one-shot, costs $6.09/case for Hit@5=72.0. A workflow-level ablation (Table 3) shows PaperPilot-9B's whole-workflow TF-IDF cosine similarity to reference workflows improves from 0.047 (base Qwen3.5-9B) to 0.362, and a sensitivity analysis finds retrieval quality peaks around candidate-pool size K=8-10 and degrades at K=20 (Recall@50 drops from 0.411 to 0.374), indicating larger first-stage pools introduce distractors rather than helping. A human study (6 participants, 5 sessions each) reports PaperPilot success rate 74.7% versus GPT-5.4's 32.0% and DeepResearch's 8.0%. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (paper search as DAG-structured workflow induction with typed operators, rather than fixed pipeline or free-language agentic reasoning) plus NEW METHOD (two-phase SFT+DPO training specifically for workflow generation and editing) and a NEW BENCHMARK (held-out multi-turn literature-search dataset with five search-direction protocols and an LLM-simulated user with leakage control). The individual retrieval operators (keyword search, citation expansion, LLM reranking, NLI filtering) are standard techniques; the novelty is in the explicit, editable, type-checked graph representation and the training recipe that teaches a 9B model to construct and locally repair such graphs under user feedback, not in any single retrieval component. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present at both retrieval level (Table 2, varying tool setup and turn count) and workflow-generation/editing level (Table 3, Figure 6-7, comparing against Qwen3.5-9B, Kimi K2.6, GPT-5.4), and a candidate-pool sensitivity sweep (Figure 8, Table 6) is reported. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed variance are reported anywhere in the main results or ablations. Code and a project website are linked (unspecified license/access details in the excerpt), which supports reproducibility of the toolset and possibly the benchmark. The paper's own retrieval and workflow-generation benchmarks were both constructed with the same broader team's protocol (citation-graph-derived gold sets, LLM-assisted synthesis) and the multi-turn evaluation depends entirely on a single simulated user model (Qwen3.5-397B) shared across all systems being compared, which is a reasonable design for fair comparison but means the headline multi-turn numbers are not validated against real human interaction except in the small separate human study. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (built system evaluated via retrieval and workflow-generation metrics). Practical: Yes — the 9B model plus symbolic toolset runs at $0.0180/case, implying feasible deployment on a single modern GPU without proprietary API access. Efficient: Substantially cheaper than GPT-5.4+Web+Toolset (8x) and DeepResearch (~340x), while retrieval quality (Hit@5=77.0) trails the best GPT-5.4 configuration (84.0) by 7 points. Effective: Evidence shows it improves Hit@5/MRR/nDCG over the untrained 9B baseline and reduces execution errors to zero, but the retrieval task is evaluated only on CS literature with hidden gold-paper sets built via a citation-graph-plus-LLM protocol, which is a reasonable proxy but not an open-ended real-world query stream. Scalable: Not tested — the paper explicitly notes the benchmark focuses on CS literature and does not test whether the toolset or trained model generalizes to other domains, larger corpora, or longer interaction horizons than the 4-5 turn cap used in evaluation. Delivers a software artifact: a project website, code repository, and trained PaperPilot-9B model/toolset (per the paper's links), plus a released benchmark protocol. Strengths: - The DAG-with-typed-operators representation is a concrete, checkable answer to the "agent tool use is hard to inspect and steer" problem, distinct from ReAct/Toolformer-style flat interleaving or DSPy/AFlow's code-level workflow compilation, by targeting paper-search-specific operators and multi-turn dialogue-conditioned editing. - The corrupted-workflow DPO construction (1,733 hard pairs from filtered teacher trajectories) is a reasonably clever way to generate hard negatives without additional human annotation, targeting the specific structural/semantic failure modes (invalid references, dropped nodes, shifted filters) that matter for this task. - The cost-effectiveness framing (CE and Rel. Eff. metrics, Table 2) makes the practical tradeoff explicit rather than only reporting top-line retrieval numbers, showing PaperPilot-9B is competitive with systems using far larger backbone models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - The predefined symbolic operator library may not cover all domain-specific search behaviors. - Workflow supervision data comes from teacher-model trajectories and may inherit the teacher's biases or blind spots. - The benchmark focuses primarily on CS literature with controlled hidden-gold evaluation, which may not reflect open-ended real-world literature search. - The multi-turn setting relies on an LLM-based user simulator; despite leakage-control mechanisms, simulated feedback cannot fully replace human interaction. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The gold-paper sets (6-15 papers per case) are constructed via citation-graph signals, human filtering, and LLM-assisted synthesis — the LLM-assisted portion is not detailed enough to rule out circularity with the LLM-based systems being evaluated. - No variance/seed information is given anywhere, so the magnitude of gains (e.g., Hit@5 58.0 to 77.0) cannot be checked against run-to-run noise. - The human study uses only 6 participants across three unrelated majors (Biology, Social Science, Computer Science) for a CS-literature-search task, which is a small and partly mismatched sample for validating generalizable claims about search quality. - The paper does not report what happens when the trained 9B model encounters search intents outside the five trained directions (predecessor/successor/sibling/benchmark/survey), leaving open how brittle the induced workflows are to novel intent categories. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating tool-augmented literature-search agents gains a concrete, well-ablated alternative to free-form agentic tool use, with strong evidence that explicit DAG workflows plus targeted SFT+DPO training let a 9B model match much of GPT-5.4's multi-turn retrieval quality at roughly 1/8 the cost, though the reliance on simulated-user evaluation for all main numbers means the human-study result (74.7% success) is the more meaningful signal and its small sample warrants a light discount.
Recommended 2607.00597 PDF
#21 — Innovative (66)

PCBWorld: A Benchmark Environment for Engine-Grounded PCB Design Automation

H Song, J Park, WS Choi, S Bae, HS Jeong, Y Park… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PCBWorld, a Gym environment that wraps KiCad's C++ PCB routing engine via 58 native Python APIs, plus PCBWorld-Bench, a benchmark of synthetic and real-world boards scored on 8 engine-checked metrics. - Why it's important: Production PCB routing still relies on decades-old rule-based routers because RL formulations collapse at fine grid resolutions and LLM one-shot generation cannot reliably satisfy hard design-rule constraints, so a benchmark that grounds agents in a real EDA engine's stepwise feedback is a prerequisite for progress. - How it does it: Agents act through the same step-level, DRC-checked API operations (start_route, make_line, make_via, finish, etc.) a human engineer uses in KiCad, receiving per-step connectivity and design-rule-check feedback rather than drawing a full board in one open-loop pass. - The good: A compact from-scratch Transformer trained with PPO only on synthetic gridless boards transfers zero-shot to 99 real open-source boards, beating all LLM baselines and approaching the strongest rule-based router (Freerouting) on clean-pass rate. - The missing: Track-removal APIs (delete_track_near, etc.) are implemented but excluded from the action space, so no agent in the paper can rip-up-and-reroute, the single most important production routing heuristic the related-work section itself cites as standard practice. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building RL or LLM agents for EDA or other constrained-design domains (CAD, chip floorplanning); the engine-grounded interaction pattern generalizes beyond PCB routing, though the workshop-paper scope means results stop at 2-8 layer boards with no component-placement co-optimization.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical/Systems profile (weighted as Empirical, since the primary claim is the zero-shot transfer finding, not a deployed tool). Novelty (68) reflects NEW SYSTEM/TOOL plus NEW BENCHMARK plus a genuine empirical finding, but no new learning algorithm. Rigor (72) is the strongest dimension: multi-seed RL results, a 3x3 reward-weight factorial ablation, an engine-engagement ablation that cleanly isolates stepwise feedback from raw model capability, current (not stale) rule-based baselines, and zero-shot D3 evaluation that avoids Goodhart on the training distribution; pulled down by single-run LLM numbers with no variance, a best-of-5 default reporting convention, and workshop-scale non-peer-review. Non-Obviousness (62) is moderate: the engine-grounded action space is well-reasoned but is the natural formulation once you decide to wrap KiCad. Completeness (72) and Resilience (62) benefit from unusually honest limitation acknowledgment (rip-up-and-reroute excluded, D3-B collapse reported rather than hidden), but the unexplained OrthoRoute near-zero CP (0.01-0.02) is a live threat to the relative-standing claim. Impact (60) reflects an open-source environment plus 5-150x faster inference, discounted because the D3-B failure (CP 0.45 vs Freerouting 0.78) means it cannot displace production routers. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*60 = 66.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 72 Resilience 62 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: PCB routing, connecting a board's electrical nets with copper traces under strict clearance and connectivity design rules, remains dominated by rule-based routers (Freerouting, OrthoRoute, KiCadRoutingTools) despite a decade of learning-based routing research. Two prior RL formulations exist: cell-by-cell movement on a discretized grid (whose search space explodes as grid resolution increases) and auxiliary-decision delegation to a fixed auto-router (net ordering, fanout target selection), neither of which draws board geometry directly. Recent LLM-based methods generate complete routing scripts or board files in a single open-loop pass without observing intermediate engine state, so they cannot use per-step design-rule feedback to correct course. No existing benchmark makes agents draw traces through a real EDA engine's native step-level operations under the engine's own DRC, the workflow an actual PCB engineer follows. Key Idea: Route generation should happen through the same fine-grained, stateful API operations (place head, draw segment, place via, release net) that the underlying EDA engine exposes to a human designer, with the engine's own DRC/connectivity feedback returned after every action, rather than through a coarse grid abstraction or a single-pass file-generation task. This closes the loop between action and engine-verified consequence at the operation level instead of the board level. Methodology: The system has four layers: (L1) the unmodified KiCad C++ engine (PCBnew core, push-and-shove router, DRC), (L2) a Python-C++ binding, (L3) a Python wrapper exposing 58 step-level APIs (25 Action, 25 State, 3 Reward, 5 Utility methods across Configuration/Routing/Dragging/Rework/Refresh groups), and (L4) a Gymnasium environment facade with four sub-modules (action dispatcher, observation builder, reward potential composer, action mask). The MDP state is a nested dictionary splitting immutable Board_static (pad geometry, design rules) from mutable Routing_geometry (tracks, vias, per-pad connectivity). Actions are (action_type, p_end, routing_mode) triples drawn from a finite engine-provided candidate pool (net_select, start_route, make_line, make_via, finish, net_end), so the continuous gridless plane is reduced to a small set of engine-meaningful points by construction. The reward is a potential-based shaping, r_t = Phi(s_{t+1}) - Phi(s_t), with Phi(s) = -(f_d(n_drv(s)) + lambda_w l(s) + lambda_v n_via(s)), where f_d is a monotone concave penalty on DRV count and lambda_w, lambda_v are user weights on wirelength and via count; per-step and terminal reward forms are equivalent under gamma=1 (used approximately with gamma=0.995 in training). Two wrappers re-encode the shared state for RL (tokenized coordinate/entity-type sequences, autoregressive action decoding with pointer-network parameter selection masked to valid actions) and for LLM agents (S-expression-style text serialization retaining only routing-relevant fields, guided-decoding grammar for free-form coordinate emission). Three design decisions stand out: (1) restricting the action space to a finite native candidate-point pool rather than a continuous plane is well-justified for tractability but forecloses novel routing geometries an unconstrained gridless agent might find; (2) excluding track-removal/rip-up APIs from the action space is acknowledged by the authors as a known limitation, not justified beyond scope-limiting; (3) using KiCad's own DRC as both the training reward source and the evaluation oracle is methodologically clean (no proxy-metric mismatch) but ties the benchmark's validity entirely to KiCad's rule coverage (35 of 62 catalogued DRC codes are used, the rest being non-routing-relevant). Key Results: On PCBWorld-Bench: (1) Synthetic grid-based boards (D1, 5 grid sizes 10-500): PPO trained per-resolution stays near-perfect single-rollout routability (Rout.@1) from grid 50 onward and only degrades past grid 200-500, while grid-cell-action baselines A2C (Jumanji) and Sable collapse by grid 100-200, because their decision horizon scales with grid cells while PPO's segment-level actions do not. (2) D2 (synthetic gridless, in-distribution test): PPO achieves Clean Pass (CP) 1.00, Potential Gain (Pot.) 10.52, matching or exceeding Freerouting (CP 1.00, Pot. 9.76) at 0.43s vs 2.69s wallclock. (3) D3-A (99 real open-source boards, zero-shot transfer from D2-trained PPO): PPO reaches CP 0.86 vs Freerouting's 0.80, still leading. (4) D3-B (10 larger real boards): Freerouting leads (CP 0.78 vs PPO's 0.45), showing PPO's generalization breaks down as net/pad counts exceed training distribution. (5) LLM agents: GPT-5.4 (interactive/engine-grounded) reaches CP 0.96 on D2 and 0.65 on D3-A, consistently beating its own plan-only (CP 0.55/0.19) and engine-free (CP 0.00/0.02) variants on the same backbone, and all LLM agents score CP 0.00 on D3-B, showing zero large-board routing capability. Runtime: GPT-5.4 interactive takes 94.04s/231.15s (D2/D3-A) vs PPO's 0.43s/1.83s, a roughly 100-200x gap. Reward-shaping ablation (3x3 sweep over lambda_w, lambda_v) shows monotone response: average wirelength drops from 484.4mm to 468.4mm as lambda_w increases, via count from 4.06 to 2.82 as lambda_v increases. Terminal-only reward variants (PPO-terminal, GRPO) underperform per-step PPO badly on the harder D3-B split (CP 0.00 and 0.10 vs PPO's 0.45), showing dense shaping matters for long-horizon credit assignment. Baselines (Freerouting, OrthoRoute, KiCadRoutingTools) are current open-source tools, not stale ones, and OrthoRoute performs very poorly (CP 0.01-0.02) across splits, an unexplained outlier the paper does not analyze. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (the engine-grounded Gym environment and its 58-API wrapper) + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (PCBWorld-Bench's D1/D2 synthetic generators and the 679-board D3 KiCad-9 port of PCBench's open-source corpus) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (that a from-scratch small Transformer trained only on synthetic boards transfers zero-shot to real boards and approaches rule-based routers). The RL algorithms (PPO, GRPO) and LLM tool-use pattern are standard; the genuine novelty is the environment design (native engine actions, per-step DRC feedback, dual RL/LLM wrappers over a shared MDP) rather than any new learning algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility: Stochastic methods (Freerouting, RL agents) report means over multiple seeds with per-seed std in Table 20 (appendix); LLM agents are evaluated with a single 5-rollout run, so no variance estimate exists for the LLM numbers, a methodological gap given LLM output stochasticity. A best-of-5-rollout selection protocol (@5) is the default reporting convention, which inflates reported numbers relative to single-shot (@1) deployment and is disclosed but should be read as an upper bound, not typical performance. Ablations are present for reward-weight sweeps (Fig. 9, 3x3 factorial) and engine-engagement levels (interactive/plan-only/engine-free, Fig. 7), both of which isolate which design choices matter. Code and PCBWorld/PCBWorld-Bench are stated as released open source, though no explicit repository URL appears in the reviewed pages. Evaluation on D3 real boards guards against the paper training and testing on the same synthetic distribution (RL trained only on D2-train, evaluated zero-shot on D3), a stronger design than Goodhart-prone same-benchmark tuning. Reference solutions for D3 come from each board's own original routed traces, an appropriate ground truth for a curated open-source corpus. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS profile (a built environment plus trained/evaluated agents). - Practical: Yes, the environment runs on the open-source KiCad engine and standard hardware; RL training uses a small from-scratch Transformer, no specialized infrastructure implied. - Efficient: PPO inference is 0.43-1.83s per board vs 2.69-7.09s for Freerouting and 94-231s for GPT-5.4, so the RL policy is roughly 5-150x faster than the alternatives it's compared against, though training cost is not reported. - Effective: CP and Pot. show PPO reaches DRV-free, near-optimal-potential routing on D2 and D3-A, a direct (not proxy) measure of the stated wirelength/via/DRC objective, since Phi(s) is the exact optimization target from Equation 1. - Scalable: Explicitly tested and found wanting: PPO's CP drops from 0.86 (D3-A, up to ~100 pads) to 0.45 (D3-B, up to 2,103 pads), and the paper is candid that this remains an open problem rather than claiming untested scalability. The work delivers a software artifact (PCBWorld environment, PCBWorld-Bench datasets and evaluation harness, trained baselines), released open source per the paper's stated intent. Strengths: - Grounding both RL and LLM agents in the identical native-API action space and engine-verified DRC removes the reward-hacking risk of hand-crafted geometric proxies, a genuine methodological improvement over prior grid-abstraction RL work. - The zero-shot synthetic-to-real transfer result (PPO trained only on D2-train reaching CP 0.86 on D3-A, second only to the strongest rule-based router) is a concrete, non-trivial empirical finding about generalization in constrained combinatorial design. - The engine-engagement ablation (interactive vs. plan-only vs. engine-free, Fig. 7) cleanly isolates the causal contribution of stepwise engine feedback versus raw model capability, a well-designed control that most LLM-for-EDA papers skip. - The DRC-code catalog and count-to-penalty mapping (Appendix F) is unusually transparent about exactly which of KiCad's 62 rule-check codes drive the reward and evaluation, reducing ambiguity about what "DRV-free" means. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper explicitly states that track-removal APIs are exposed but excluded from the current action space, precluding rip-up-and-reroute refinement that high-quality routing typically relies on. It also states current LLM agents show poor scalability to complex boards (CP 0.00 on D3-B) and that component placement is fixed rather than jointly optimized with routing, both flagged as future work. The paper directly confronts these gaps rather than glossing over them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - OrthoRoute's near-zero CP (0.01-0.02) across all splits is reported without explanation or discussion; if this reflects a misconfiguration rather than a genuine capability gap, it artificially inflates PCBWorld agents' relative standing against "rule-based routers." - LLM agents are evaluated with only a single 5-rollout run (no seed variance), unlike the RL and rule-based baselines, making cross-method statistical comparison on LLM numbers unreliable. - The benchmark's design-rule surface is fixed to KiCad's DRC catalog; industrial routing objectives the paper itself names as unaddressed (signal integrity, EMI, thermal) are not in Phi(s) or the evaluation metrics, so "DRV-free" routing here is a narrower notion of correctness than production PCB sign-off. - No training compute cost, wall-clock training time, or comparison of RL sample efficiency is reported, so the practical cost of reproducing the from-scratch Transformer result is unstated. - All results come from a single workshop-scale study by one industry lab (LG AI Research) with no independent replication; as a KDD workshop paper it has not undergone full peer review. Bottom Line: Worth reading for anyone building RL or LLM routing agents or engine-grounded benchmarks for constrained physical design, primarily for the environment design pattern and the synthetic-to-real zero-shot transfer result, but the D3-B large-board collapse (both RL and LLM CP near zero) means the paper documents an open problem more than it solves one.
Search Results 2607.05915 PDF
#22 — Innovative (65)

Parallelized Autoregressive Decoding for Omni-Modal Dense Video Captioning (16 ▲ )

Wenzheng Zeng, Siyi Jiao, Chen Gao, Hwee Tou Ng, Mike Zheng Shou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 8 Jul 2026
