Bottom Line: A practitioner operating disaggregated LLM-serving infrastructure gains a concrete, code-available technique and a genuinely useful empirical diagnosis (prefill queue wait and KV transfer, not prefill compute, drive tail latency) that is not covered as precisely by prior disaggregation papers (Splitwise, DistServe, TaiChi), though the single-model, single-topology, no-error-bar evaluation means the reported 81% TTFT reduction should be read as a best-case number from one trace rather than a generalizable guarantee.
Search Results 2607.02043 PDF
#28 — Innovative (62)

WattGPU: Predicting Inference Power and Latency on Unseen GPUs and LLMs

MF Argerich, J Fürst, M Patiño-Martínez · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces two gradient-boosted regression models that predict mean GPU power draw and inter-token latency (ITL) for arbitrary LLM-GPU inference pairs, trained solely on publicly available LLM metadata and GPU specifications with zero hardware profiling. - Why it's important: Small- and medium-scale operators lack the hardware access and budget to exhaustively profile every candidate LLM-GPU combination, yet GPU choice alone can cut power draw by 43% for the same model under the same latency SLA (e.g., Llama 3.1 8B on an A30 vs. an H100). - How it does it: XGBoost regressors trained on GPU specs (TDP, memory bandwidth, clock rates) and Hugging Face metadata (parameter count, layers, attention heads), plus three engineered physically grounded features (boosting ratio, bandwidth latency, compute latency), with the power target normalized by each GPU's TDP to make the learned behavior scale-invariant across architectures. - The good: Under Leave-One-GPU-Out cross-validation on completely unseen GPUs, the power model reaches 3.4% (offline) / 13.5% (server) median absolute percentage error (MdAPE) and the ITL model reaches 8.5% (server), beating load-scaled TDP and roofline baselines by roughly 2-4x while preserving GPU ranking quality (Kendall tau greater than or equal to 0.76 for server scenarios). - The missing: Offline-scenario ITL prediction is markedly weaker (24.9% MdAPE under Leave-One-GPU-Out) and the entire evaluation is restricted to 8 dense-model-only NVIDIA GPUs and 42 dense LLMs up to 27B parameters, excluding Mixture-of-Experts, quantized, and multi-GPU deployments entirely. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building capacity-planning or energy-reporting tooling for LLM inference fleets; the ranking-quality results (Kendall tau) matter more than the point-error numbers for that use case, and the offline-ITL weakness should temper expectations for batch-serving deployments.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (68) and Completeness (72) are the strengths: the three-tier CV/LOGO/LOLO protocol directly targets the generalization question rather than a single split, code and the Watt Counts dataset are publicly released, and the paper honestly reports that the roofline baseline out-ranks its learned ITL model on Kendall tau under LOGO despite 3x worse absolute error. Novelty (55) and Non-Obviousness (50) hold the composite back — TDP normalization plus two physics-derived features on stock XGBoost is a sensible but unsurprising recipe, and no learned baseline from the cited related work (Fu et al.'s GNN) is benchmarked, so it is unclear how much of the gain is the method versus being first to test LOGO/LOLO at all. Scope is narrow: 8 NVIDIA GPUs, 42 dense models, no MoE/quantized/multi-GPU.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 72 Resilience 62 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Operators running small- to medium-scale LLM inference deployments (as opposed to hyperscalers) need to select which GPU to pair with a given model to minimize power draw and meet latency service-level agreements (SLAs), but doing so today requires physically profiling each candidate (GPU, LLM) combination — a process requiring diverse hardware access, specialized expertise, and nontrivial cost that most such operators lack. Existing energy/latency benchmarking and prediction efforts (MLPerf Power, ML.ENERGY, TokenPowerBench, EcoLogits, and several regression/GNN-based predictors) all either require profiling the exact target hardware or have not been evaluated for generalization to GPUs unseen during training, leaving a gap for pre-deployment estimation on genuinely novel hardware. Key Idea: Physically motivated feature engineering plus target normalization lets a standard tabular regressor generalize across GPU architectures it has never seen: normalizing the power-draw target by each GPU's Thermal Design Power (TDP) converts an absolute, hardware-scale-dependent quantity into a relative, scale-invariant one the model can learn from a small set of training GPUs and extrapolate to new ones, while engineered features (bandwidth latency = model-weight bytes / memory bandwidth; compute latency = 2*params / FLOPS) inject known first-order physics (LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound) that the model can then correct with residual patterns. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages: (1) feature extraction, pulling GPU specs (TDP, memory bandwidth, clock rates, transistor count, memory type/size, FP16 TFLOPS) from manufacturer data and LLM architecture fields (parameter count, layer count, attention/KV heads, hidden size, model type) from Hugging Face metadata, then deriving boosting ratio, bandwidth latency, and compute latency; (2) two independent XGBoost regressors — one for mean power draw P-bar (target normalized by GPU TDP, max_depth=6, n_estimators=100), one for ITL (max_depth=5, n_estimators=200), both with reg_lambda=100 chosen via grid search over depth in {3..7}, lambda in {0.1,...,500}, and n_estimators in {50,...,500}; (3) evaluation via three splitting strategies of increasing difficulty — 5-fold GroupKFold CV (unseen GPU-LLM combination of known GPUs/LLMs), Leave-One-GPU-Out (LOGO, unseen GPU), and Leave-One-LLM-Out (LOLO, unseen LLM). Two design decisions stand out: normalizing power by TDP is well justified by the strong TDP-vs-max-power correlation and is empirically validated by the GPU-based features (memory bandwidth, TDP-related) dominating feature importance; algorithm selection (XGBoost over linear/elastic-net/MLP) is justified only by MdAPE/Pearson-r comparison during LOGO CV, not by a stated theoretical argument, so the choice is empirically but not mechanistically justified. Key Results: On the Watt Counts dataset (8 server-grade NVIDIA GPUs — V100, T4, A100, A30, L40S, L4, H100, H200 — and 42 dense LLMs from 0.124B to 27B parameters), the power model achieves MdAPE of 1.6% (CV), 3.4% (LOGO, unseen GPU), 2.0% (LOLO, unseen LLM) offline, and 5.5% / 13.5% / 6.7% server, versus 4.4% (offline) and 26.0% (server) for the best TDP-scaled baseline — a roughly 4x reduction on unseen (GPU, LLM) combinations for server scenarios and roughly 2x on completely unseen GPUs, with GPU Kendall tau greater than or equal to 0.86 versus 0.60 for the baseline. The ITL model achieves MdAPE of 12.8% (CV), 24.9% (LOGO), 15.6% (LOLO) offline, and 4.9% / 8.5% / 5.6% server, versus a roofline baseline at 80.2% (offline) and 29.6% (server); the roofline baseline actually matches or exceeds the learned model's GPU ranking quality (tau) under LOGO in both scenarios despite 3x higher absolute error, indicating the two approaches are complementary rather than one strictly dominating. Baselines are physically grounded (TDP-scaled, roofline-derived) rather than the strongest possible ML competitors — no comparison is made against the graph-neural-network approach of Fu et al. or other learned baselines from related work, only against non-learned analytical ones. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (feature engineering + TDP-normalized XGBoost regression for cross-hardware generalization) combined with a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (the first quantified generalization results for LLM power/latency prediction on GPUs unseen during training); the underlying learning algorithm (XGBoost) and general regression-for-hardware-performance-prediction approach are standard, so the novelty is concentrated in the normalization strategy, the domain-specific derived features, and the rigorous LOGO/LOLO evaluation protocol rather than in the modeling technique itself. Rigor & Reproducibility: Feature importance analysis (Figure 3) substitutes for a formal ablation, showing scenario and memory-bandwidth features dominate for power while bandwidth latency dominates for ITL, but no leave-one-feature-out ablation quantifies each feature's marginal contribution to MdAPE. No error bars, seeds, or significance tests are reported for the XGBoost results themselves (only ±1 standard deviation bars on feature-importance and power-draw-distribution figures, not on the headline MdAPE/Pearson-r/Kendall-tau numbers), so it is not possible to assess whether a 3.4% vs. 5.5% MdAPE difference is statistically distinguishable from noise across folds. Code and the Watt Counts training/evaluation dataset are publicly released at https://github.com/maufadel/wattgpu, which is a genuine reproducibility positive. The baselines are analytical and hyperparameter-free, so there is no risk of the baseline being under-tuned relative to the proposed model; the evaluation strategy (LOGO/LOLO) directly targets and mitigates the standard risk of a technique being validated only on the same distribution it was fit to. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: EMPIRICAL (regression models evaluated via cross-validated held-out prediction). Practical: Yes — the method requires only publicly available GPU spec sheets and Hugging Face metadata at inference time, no target hardware access, so it runs anywhere a spreadsheet of specs is available. Efficient: Training and inference cost is that of a small XGBoost model (100-200 trees, depth 5-6) over roughly 42 LLMs times 8 GPUs times a handful of scenarios — negligible compared to any actual profiling run, though the paper reports no explicit training-time or inference-time figures. Effective: The evidence (MdAPE, Pearson r, Kendall tau against held-out GPUs and LLMs) directly measures the stated problem (predicting power/latency for unseen hardware), not a proxy, though the offline-ITL result (24.9% MdAPE under LOGO) shows the method only partially solves the problem in that regime. Scalable: Not tested — the paper evaluates 8 GPUs and 42 dense models up to 27B parameters and explicitly defers Mixture-of-Experts, quantized, and multi-GPU configurations to future work, so behavior at 10x-100x model scale (e.g., 100B+ parameter or multi-GPU-sharded models) is unevaluated and unclaimed. Strengths: - Rigorous three-tier evaluation protocol (CV / LOGO / LOLO) that explicitly isolates generalization to unseen GPU-LLM pairs, unseen GPUs, and unseen LLMs, rather than a single train-test split, is a genuine methodological strength uncommon in prior LLM-energy-prediction work cited in Section 2. - The TDP-normalization trick for the power target is a simple, well-motivated design choice with a clear physical justification (TDP correlates with long-term max power) and is shown empirically to enable cross-architecture transfer. - Honest complementary-baseline framing: the paper does not oversell the learned model as strictly superior, explicitly noting the roofline baseline out-ranks the learned ITL model's GPU tau under LOGO despite worse absolute error. - Public release of code and the underlying Watt Counts dataset supports independent verification. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that offline-scenario ITL prediction is markedly harder (24.9% LOGO MdAPE) and attribute this to static metadata features being unable to capture vLLM's continuous-batching dynamics; they explicitly scope the work to dense models in single-GPU configurations, deferring Mixture-of-Experts, quantized models, and multi-GPU deployments as future work. This is a direct, non-minimizing acknowledgment of scope boundaries. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No comparison against any learned baseline from cited related work (e.g., the GNN-based approach of Fu et al. or the ML-based per-prompt predictor of Caravaca et al.), so it is unclear how much of the reported improvement is attributable to the modeling approach versus simply being the first to test LOGO/LOLO generalization at all. - The dataset spans only NVIDIA GPUs from a single vendor across 2018-2024 architectures (Volta through Hopper); generalization to AMD accelerators, TPUs, or genuinely next-generation architectures (e.g., Blackwell) with different power/frequency-scaling behavior is neither tested nor discussed as a limitation. - No confidence intervals or repeated-fold variance are reported for the headline MdAPE/tau numbers, so apparent differences between scenarios or against baselines cannot be checked for statistical robustness. - The claimed real-world energy-savings example (Figure 1, 43% power reduction by choosing A30 over H100 for Llama 3.1 8B) is presented as a demonstration rather than as part of the held-out evaluation, so its accuracy is not independently stress-tested beyond the aggregate CV/LOGO/LOLO numbers. Bottom Line: A practitioner selecting GPUs for LLM inference deployment gains a genuinely validated, code-released tool for ranking and estimating power/latency without profiling every candidate combination, though the offline-ITL weakness and the narrow (dense-model, single-GPU, NVIDIA-only) evaluation scope mean it should be treated as a strong first step rather than a deployment-ready universal predictor.
Search Results 2607.02391 PDF
#29 — Innovative (61)

Vidu S1: A Real-Time Interactive Video Generation Model (108 ▲ )

Jintao Zhang, Kai Jiang, Jintao Chen, Xu Wang, Yang Luo, Yuji Wang, Dechuang Chen, Jungang Li, Chengyang Ye, Marco Chen, Hongzhou Zhu, Min Zhao, Yuxuan Jiang, Zhengkun Huang, Chendong Xiang, Kaiwen Zheng, Haoxu Wang, Xiaohang Wang, Qi Jia, Xin Chen, Yimin Chen, Youhe Jiang, Fangcheng Fu, Zhijie Deng, Fan Bao, Jianfei Chen, Jun Zhu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Vidu S1, a real-time interactive video generation model that produces speech-controllable, infinite-length 540p avatar video at 42 FPS on a single consumer GPU. - Why it's important: Existing video generation models (Sora, Veo, Wan, Seedance) are offline, one-shot systems that force users to wait minutes for a fixed output, which blocks live streaming, gaming, and conversational avatar use cases that need immediate, ongoing user control. - How it does it: A three-stage training pipeline (bidirectional teacher, causal autoregressive adaptation via Teacher Forcing/Diffusion Forcing, then Distribution Matching Distillation with Phased Consistency Model regularization) paired with a sliding-window inference scheme (persistent reference context plus TwinCache dual noisy/clean history caching) that bounds error accumulation over arbitrarily long streams. - The good: On the authors' Vidu-StreamBench (500 samples), Vidu S1 hits 100% human-preference win rate on subject controllability against HeyGen and LemonSlice, and on HDTF it posts the best CSIM (0.9192), Sync-D (7.847), and DOVER (0.5660) among 8 compared methods while being the only one that is simultaneously instruction-following, real-time, and highest-resolution (540p at 42 FPS). - The missing: There is no ablation isolating the contribution of TwinCache, RoPE repositioning, or the PCM regularization term individually, so it is unclear how much each inference-side or training-side component contributes to the reported quality and speed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building real-time avatar or streaming-video systems who need a concrete recipe for causal distillation plus sliding-window caching; skip if the interest is offline high-fidelity video generation quality, since this paper trades some of that for latency.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (70) and Novelty (60) hold the composite up, while Rigor (45) drags it down. TwinCache's noisy/clean dual-cache scheduling is a concrete mechanism, not a relabeled KV cache, and the 42 FPS / 540p / instruction-following combination on a consumer RTX 5090 is a genuinely rare operating point; but the artifact is a hosted demo, not code, weights, or the benchmark, which caps Impact well below L002's full open release. Rigor (45) is undercut by three compounding problems: no ablation isolates TwinCache, RoPE repositioning, or the PCM term, so the paper cannot say which component produces the result; the primary benchmark (Vidu-StreamBench, 500 samples) is in-house, unreleased, and the source of the headline 100% subject-controllability win rate — a textbook Goodhart setup; and no variance is reported, making the CSIM win of 0.9192 vs. HeyGen's 0.9191 statistically meaningless as presented. Resilience (50) is low because the 'infinite-length, drift-free' claim rests on one 90-second qualitative figure with no drift-over-time curve. Completeness (55) gets partial credit for the honest DMD-instability admission and for running human A/B tests rather than relying only on self-reported metrics, but the final model's failure modes are never discussed. Weighted: 0.15*60 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.30*70 = 61.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Live streaming, gaming, video calls, and virtual-host applications need video that responds to user input as it is generated, but state-of-the-art video diffusion models (Sora, Veo, Wan, Seedance) generate offline in a single shot, with users passively waiting minutes for a complete clip and no ability to intervene mid-generation. The paper motivates the demand asymmetry with a simple argument: interactive-content demand scales as alphaN (N users) while offline-content demand scales as betaN/m because content gets reused m times, so when m > 100 the market for real-time interactive video vastly exceeds offline video. Prior autoregressive video models close part of the gap but still fall short in one of four ways: they lack live user interaction during generation, they don't use speech as a direct control signal, they suffer drift and visual collapse over long horizons, and they lack the inference/serving infrastructure needed to actually run in real time. Key Idea: The core trick is decoupling temporal propagation from appearance refinement during streaming inference via TwinCache: the historical context is represented by a noisy cache (preserving coarse temporal dynamics as an implicit low-pass filter that suppresses high-frequency artifact accumulation) during intermediate denoising steps, and swapped for a clean cache (restoring fine-grained visual detail) only at the final denoising step. Combined with a persistent first-frame reference context acting as an attention sink, this bounds drift over open-ended streams without requiring the model to re-observe the entire history. Methodology: The pipeline has three training stages and two inference-time components. Stage 1 trains a bidirectional "teacher" diffusion model on full video-audio sequences with a standard denoising-prediction loss (Eq. 3) to establish a generative prior. Stage 2 initializes a causal "student" from that teacher and imposes a causal attention mask, training with a stochastic mixture (Bernoulli(p)) of Teacher Forcing (clean ground-truth history, tau=0) and Diffusion Forcing (noisy history, tau>0) to close the train/inference gap for autoregressive rollout (Eq. 5). Stage 3 applies Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) to compress many-step generation into a few-step generator (Eq. 6), regularized by a Phased Consistency Model (PCM) perceptual-distance loss (Eq. 7) to counter the mode collapse, camera drift, and temporal inconsistency the authors report DMD alone produces. At inference, sliding-window attention restricts each step to three components (persistent reference, cached history within the window, current denoising target), with RoPE repositioning applied to cached historical key-value features to preserve positional consistency as the window advances, avoiding recomputation. The inference stack further layers on SageAttention/SpargeAttention/SLA kernels, custom per-block W8A8 quantized GEMM, Triton/CUDA kernel fusion, CUDA graphs, and Ulysses-style multi-GPU context parallelism (building on the authors' own TurboDiffusion and TurboServe systems). Critical design decisions: (1) the Teacher Forcing/Diffusion Forcing mixture is well-motivated as a standard fix for exposure bias in autoregressive diffusion, consistent with prior work (Diffusion Forcing, cited); (2) the DMD+PCM combination is empirically justified in-paper (the text states DMD alone caused instability, motivating PCM addition) but no ablation quantifies the PCM contribution; (3) the choice of per-block (rather than per-tensor/per-channel) quantization granularity is justified by an outlier-sensitivity argument but not benchmarked against alternatives in this paper. Key Results: On the authors' own Vidu-StreamBench (500 in-house samples with action instructions, reference frames, and audio), pairwise human A/B preference tests show Vidu S1 preferred overall 56% vs. 28% against HeyGen, 46% vs. 30% against LemonSlice, and 48% vs. 30% against Kling-Avatar-2.0, with a 100% preference win on subject controllability against HeyGen and LemonSlice (60% vs. 16% against Kling-Avatar-2.0). On the public HDTF benchmark, Vidu S1 reports CSIM 0.9192 (best among 8 methods, next best HeyGen at 0.9191), Sync-D 7.847 (best, next best LemonSlice at 7.921), and DOVER 0.5660 (best, next best LiveAvatar at 0.5639), while being the only method that is simultaneously instruction-following and real-time, at the highest reported resolution (540p, 960x540) and highest FPS (42, next fastest HeyGen at 25 FPS, measured on RTX 5090 GPUs). Baselines span both open-source autoregressive models (Wan2.2-S2V-14B, Hallo3, StableAvatar-1.3B, OmniAvatar-1.3B, LiveAvatar, LemonSlice) and commercial systems (HeyGen, Kling-Avatar-2.0), which is a reasonably current and strong comparison set for this narrow subfield as of the paper's writing. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW METHOD (TwinCache), ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION, NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (Vidu-StreamBench). The genuinely novel piece is TwinCache's noisy/clean dual-cache scheduling for streaming diffusion inference and the combination of DMD+PCM for causal distillation in this domain; the three-stage bidirectional-to-causal-to-distilled training recipe and the Teacher/Diffusion Forcing mixture are standard combinations of existing techniques (Diffusion Forcing, DMD, PCM, attention sinks) applied and integrated for a new real-time avatar setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablation study isolates the individual contributions of TwinCache, RoPE repositioning, the PCM regularization term, or the choice of Teacher/Diffusion Forcing mixture ratio p; the paper asserts these design choices work but does not measure each independently. No error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported for either the CSIM/Sync-D/DOVER metrics or the human preference percentages. The primary benchmark, Vidu-StreamBench, is authored in-house by the same team and not independently validated or released for external scrutiny (no stated public release); this is a Goodhart's Law risk since the model that wins 100% on subject controllability was evaluated on the developers' own instruction set. HDTF results provide a partial independent check, and the paper does report human A/B preference tests rather than relying solely on self-reported automatic metrics, which is a partial mitigation. No code, weights, or dataset release URL is stated beyond a playable online demo link (https://vidu.com/vidu-stream); this is a demo, not a reproducibility artifact. Deployability (4-property check): Profile used: SYSTEMS (a built, deployed model plus serving stack, evaluated by real-time throughput and human preference on a streaming task). - Practical: Yes, demonstrated running on RTX 5090 consumer GPUs, not requiring a data-center cluster for single-user inference. - Efficient: The paper reports 42 FPS at 540p with a 3-step generator, aided by W8A8 quantization, kernel fusion, CUDA graphs, and sparse/low-bit attention, but gives no absolute latency, memory footprint, or cost-per-hour figures, nor a head-to-head compute-cost comparison against baselines beyond the FPS column in Table 1. - Effective: Human preference tests and standard identity/sync/quality metrics on HDTF and Vidu-StreamBench show the model beats compared systems on most axes, indicating it solves the stated interactive avatar problem rather than only a loosely related proxy, though the in-house benchmark limits independent verification. - Scalable: Multi-GPU Ulysses-style context parallelism is described for cluster-level serving, but no experiment reports throughput or latency at 10x-100x concurrent users or longer time horizons than tested; "infinite-length" generation is demonstrated qualitatively (Figure 1 shows a 90-second sequence) but not stress-tested at, e.g., multi-hour duration with quantitative drift measurements. The paper delivers a playable online demo (https://vidu.com/vidu-stream) as a product artifact rather than open code, weights, or the Vidu-StreamBench dataset. Strengths: - TwinCache's noisy/clean dual-cache decoupling is a concrete, well-motivated mechanism for balancing long-term temporal coherence against per-frame visual fidelity in streaming diffusion, not just a relabeling of existing KV-caching. - The demand-scaling argument in the introduction (alphaN vs. betaN/m) gives a clear, quantifiable business case for why real-time interactive generation is a distinct problem from offline generation, rather than an unsupported motivational claim. - The full-stack integration, from data pipeline through causal distillation to custom CUDA kernels and multi-GPU serving, is executed coherently enough to hit a genuinely rare combination: highest resolution, highest FPS, and instruction-following, simultaneously, among the compared systems. - The 6-stage data filtering pipeline (Figure 2) combining expert models with an omni-model semantic filter is a practically useful recipe for building clean, diarized, captioned single-subject video-audio training data. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that optimizing solely with the DMD objective causes mode collapse, camera drift, content degeneration, and temporal inconsistency, which motivated adding PCM regularization; this is a direct, credible acknowledgment of a real failure mode encountered during development. Beyond this training-stability limitation, the paper does not discuss failure cases of the final deployed model (e.g., under extreme poses, non-human subjects, adversarial or out-of-distribution speech) or state boundary conditions under which Vidu S1 itself might drift or fail. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No ablation isolates TwinCache, RoPE repositioning, or PCM's contribution to final quality; all evidence for these components is qualitative narrative, not controlled experiments. - Vidu-StreamBench is an in-house, unreleased benchmark authored by the same team that built the model being evaluated on it, which is the paper's primary quantitative testbed (500 samples) and the source of its most striking claim (100% preference win rate on subject controllability); no external benchmark corroborates this specific claim. - "Infinite-length" and "drift-free" claims are demonstrated by a 90-second qualitative example (Figure 1) and design arguments about the sliding window, not by a quantitative drift measurement (e.g., identity similarity or Sync-D) plotted over a multi-minute or multi-hour generation. - No compute cost, GPU-hour, or dollar-cost comparison against baselines is given despite "low-cost GPU" being a headline claim in the abstract and Figure 1. - The paper does not report variance or confidence intervals on any metric, making it hard to judge whether the narrow wins (e.g., CSIM 0.9192 vs. HeyGen's 0.9191) are meaningful. Bottom Line: Engineers building real-time, speech-driven avatar or streaming-video systems will find TwinCache and the causal-distillation training recipe directly useful and reasonably well-specified, but the headline benchmark numbers rest on an unreleased in-house dataset and lack the ablations needed to know which components are doing the work.
Recommended 2607.03118 PDF
#30 — Innovative (60)

AscendKernelGen: LLM-Driven Kernel Generation for NPUs

X Cao, J Zhai, P Li, Z Hu, C Yan, G Fang, B She, J Li… · aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AscendKernelGen, a generation-evaluation framework that pairs a reasoning dataset (Ascend-CoT), a domain-adapted model (KernelGen-LM) trained via SFT plus RL, and a hardware-grounded benchmark (NPUKernelBench) to generate compilable and functionally correct kernels for Huawei Ascend NPUs. - Why it's important: NPU kernel development requires the proprietary AscendC DSL with explicit memory hierarchy management, async pipelining, and boundary-sensitive arithmetic, and general-purpose LLMs achieve near-zero execution success on complex kernels, making kernel optimization a bottleneck for deploying AI on emerging accelerators. - How it does it: A three-tier chain-of-thought corpus (documentation-based, kernel/project-level code-centric, general reasoning) drives SFT, followed by error-derived supervision mined from real compilation and execution failures, then DPO-based reinforcement learning over Green/Orange/Purple (correct/executable-but-wrong/non-compiling) preference pairs. - The good: On Level 2 (structured NN-operator) kernels, Pass@10 compilation rate rises from 0% (base Qwen3-32B) to 96.54% and execution rate from 0% to 40.48%, with RL-tuned kernels achieving 1.86x speedup over vendor-optimized baselines at Pass@100. - The missing: Level 3 kernels (Gemm, TopK, attention-style global-dependency operators) remain largely unsolved, topping out at 16.67% execution rate even at Pass@100 after SFT+RL, and the paper reports no comparison against any competing LLM-kernel-generation baseline (e.g., a CUDA-oriented RL pipeline ported to Ascend, or a prompting-only frontier model) run on the same benchmark. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building domain-adaptive code-generation pipelines for non-CUDA accelerators or designing error-derived RL supervision from compiler/execution feedback; skip if the interest is CUDA/Triton kernel generation, since the AscendC DSL and hardware are platform-specific and the authors state the pipeline is not directly transferable.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Impact (70) carries most of the weight: released dataset (Ascend-CoT), model (KernelGen-LM), and benchmark (NPUKernelBench), all validated by execution on real Ascend hardware, with 1.86x speedup over vendor kernels on Level 2. Rigor (55) and Resilience (50) are the drags — the paper compares only against its own zero-shot baseline with no competing LLM-kernel-generation system re-run on NPUKernelBench, the benchmark is built by the same team that built the training data with no stated decontamination check, and the abstract's headline '64% functional correctness' is not traceable to any row in Table 2. Level 3 kernels (Gemm, TopK, attention), the tier that actually matters for deployment, remain at 16.67% execution rate even at Pass@100, so effectiveness claims are scoped to L1/L2.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 60 Resilience 50 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: NPU accelerators (specifically Huawei Ascend) require kernels written in AscendC, a proprietary DSL demanding explicit control of hierarchical memory (global vs. on-chip), manual tiling for boundary-misaligned tensors, asynchronous pipeline programming with hand-inserted synchronization primitives, and direct use of vector (SIMD) and matrix (Cube) execution units. This is the domain of NPU kernel engineers and ML infrastructure teams targeting Ascend hardware. General-purpose and even CUDA/Triton-oriented LLM kernel-generation approaches fail here because (a) they hallucinate non-existent APIs (e.g., a fabricated `AscendC::Softmax`) or misuse core interfaces, and (b) high-quality AscendC training data is scarce relative to CUDA, leaving a domain gap that prompting alone cannot close. Key Idea: Layer domain-adaptive training with an error-derived supervision signal mined directly from real compilation and execution failures (not just static code), then use those corrected-but-imperfect kernels to build execution-graded preference pairs (Green > Orange/Purple) for RL, rather than relying on purely static SFT data or generic outcome-only reward signals. The compounding insight is that eliminating catastrophic execution failures via targeted SFT correction first narrows the RL search space, making subsequent preference learning focus on fine-grained execution-quality distinctions (memory access pattern, accumulation order, numerical stability) rather than recovering from broken code. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Ascend-CoT dataset construction (83,916 raw samples, distilled to 9,955 32k-token SFT sequences after length filtering): documentation-based CoT via a knowledge-instruct paradigm applied to AscendC manuals (DeepSeek-R1 generation); code-centric CoT split into kernel-level (single-file reasoning about tiling/memory/pipeline choices) and project-level (host-kernel decomposition using Gemini 2.5 Pro for multi-file dependency reasoning); and general CoT from open-source math/code/science corpora for transfer retention. (2) KernelGen-LM training: error-derived SFT injects two correction types — API-level fixes from clustered compiler error logs augmented with retrieved documentation, and kernel-level fixes via ground-truth-guided reconstruction against reference implementations for silent numerical bugs — followed by DPO-based RL using pairwise preferences over three outcome tiers (fully correct / executable-but-wrong / compile-failure) sampled from the SFT model on real Ascend hardware. (3) NPUKernelBench: 158 tasks across 16 categories and three complexity levels (L1 element-wise/arithmetic, L2 structured NN operators, L3 global-dependency/dynamic-control kernels like Gemm/TopK), evaluated under both static-shape and dynamic-shape settings, using pass@k for Compilation Rate and Execution Rate plus Speedup against vendor-optimized baselines. Critical design choices: (i) using compile-pass-but-execution-fail samples (not compile-fail samples) as RL negatives, justified empirically in an ablation (Table 4) as more informative; (ii) full fine-tuning over LoRA, justified by a direct comparison (55.32%/22.13% vs. 40.29%/13.55% mean CR/ER); (iii) discarding SFT samples over 32k tokens rather than truncating, which is reasonable for preventing corrupted long-context training but is not itself ablated. Key Results: On Qwen3-32B, mean Pass@1 compilation rate improves from 25.59% (base) to 71.89% (SFT) to 70.35% (SFT+RL, roughly flat vs. SFT), while mean Pass@1 execution rate improves from 11.59% to 27.31% to 32.04%. At Pass@100, mean CR reaches 100% and mean ER reaches 88.89% for SFT+RL, with the abstract's headline figure (95.5% Pass@10 compilation, 64% functional correctness) referring to Level-2-specific Pass@10 numbers (96.54% CR / 40.48% ER) rather than the aggregate — a framing worth flagging since the abstract's "64%" correctness figure does not appear verbatim in Table 2's main results and is not reconciled in the visible text. Speedup over vendor baselines: SFT reaches 1.50x on Level 2, RL pushes this to 1.86x, while Level 1 stays around 0.6x (base) or 0.61x (RL) — the model does not out-optimize experts on the simplest kernels. Level 3 tasks remain essentially unsolved: 0% execution rate at Pass@100 for the base model and SFT, rising to only 16.67% under SFT+RL. Baselines are the zero-shot performance of Qwen3-8B, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, Llama3.1-8B, and Mistral-7B (Table 1), all near 0% functional correctness on complex kernels — reasonable domain baselines, though there is no comparison to any other published LLM-kernel-generation system (CUDA-focused RL pipelines, agentic kernel frameworks) adapted to this benchmark, so the paper's improvement is measured only against its own zero-shot baseline, not against competing methods. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (Ascend-CoT and NPUKernelBench are stated as the first CoT dataset and first benchmark in the NPU kernel domain) and NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (KernelGen-LM as a released domain-adapted model), combined with an ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION contribution in the error-derived SFT + execution-preference RL training recipe. The training recipe itself (SFT on CoT data, DPO-style RL on execution-graded preferences, error-log-mined correction data) is a recombination of established techniques (knowledge-instruct distillation, DPO, compiler-feedback-augmented training) rather than a new algorithm; the genuine novelty is applying and validating this combination specifically for the NPU/AscendC setting where no prior comprehensive training or evaluation infrastructure existed. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are reasonably thorough: model scale (1.7B-32B, Figure 4), FFT vs. LoRA (Table 3), training data composition — kernel-centric vs. documentation vs. general CoT removed individually (Figure 5a) — training data ratio scaling (Figure 5b), and RL negative-sampling strategy plus LR schedule (Table 4). Each configuration is run at least three times on real NPU hardware with mean values reported, and the paper states compilation/correctness outcomes were consistent across trials while runtime metrics showed only minor variation — a positive for reproducibility, though no explicit variance/error bars are shown in the tables or figures themselves, so the claimed stability cannot be independently checked from the given pages. Code, dataset (Ascend-CoT), model (KernelGen-LM), and benchmark (NPUKernelBench) are stated as publicly available with named repository links. A methodological concern: NPUKernelBench is built by the same team that builds the training data and model, and while task specifications appear held out as an evaluation harness rather than training targets, the paper does not explicitly state a decontamination check confirming zero overlap between Ascend-CoT training kernels and the 158 NPUKernelBench tasks — this should be verified before treating the benchmark numbers as fully held-out. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a released framework: dataset, trained model, and benchmark, evaluated by execution on real hardware). - Practical: Requires actual Ascend NPU hardware for both training-loop reward computation and benchmark evaluation — the paper's own Limitations section acknowledges this raises the barrier to entry for researchers without comparable hardware access. - Efficient: Not stated in visible pages — no training compute cost, GPU/NPU-hours, or inference latency for the generation pipeline itself is reported; only the generated kernels' runtime speedup is measured. - Effective: Strong evidence for L1/L2 kernels (near-100% Pass@100 compilation, up to 91.67% Pass@100 execution rate on L2), but the paper's own results show the approach does not solve the hardest tier (L3: 16.67% Pass@100 execution rate), so "effective" claims should be scoped to L1/L2 complexity. - Scalable: The paper does test scale directly via a model-size sweep (1.7B-32B) and reports that only 32B shows any non-trivial L3 compilation success, suggesting the current recipe does not scale down well and may still be capacity-limited even at 32B; extrapolation to substantially larger models or broader operator coverage is not tested. Delivers a software artifact: released dataset (Ascend-CoT), trained model (KernelGen-LM), and benchmark (NPUKernelBench), not merely a paper-form technique. Strengths: - Error-derived supervision that mines real compiler and execution failures into targeted correction data is a concrete, demonstrated mechanism (not just claimed) for closing the SFT-to-RL gap, evidenced by the negative-sampling ablation in Table 4. - The Level 1/2/3 task stratification by algorithmic dependency structure (Table 5) gives a genuinely diagnostic benchmark rather than a single aggregate pass rate, exposing exactly where current LLMs fail (global dependencies, dynamic control flow). - Speedup evaluation against vendor-optimized baselines (not just correctness) is a meaningful practical-impact axis that many kernel-generation papers omit. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly: (1) the pipeline is platform-specific to Ascend/AscendC and transferring to other accelerators would require substantial re-collection of domain data, benchmark redesign, and compiler/runtime adaptation; (2) NPUKernelBench's standardized templates and controlled interfaces do not capture the full variability of open-ended, real-world operator development with heterogeneous dependencies; (3) the training/evaluation pipeline requires compiler-backed, execution-based verification on real NPU hardware, raising the barrier to reproduction; (4) the current alignment procedure optimizes correctness but not global performance (latency, tiling efficiency, hardware utilization) as a primary RL objective, and generalization to more complex or unseen kernel patterns with long-range dependencies remains constrained by training-distribution coverage. This is a direct, non-minimizing acknowledgment of limitations, a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No comparison against any other LLM-based kernel-generation method (CUDA-oriented RL frameworks, agentic kernel-debugging systems cited in Related Work) adapted or re-run on NPUKernelBench; all comparisons are against the paper's own zero-shot baseline and vendor-hand-written kernels, not competing learned approaches. - The abstract's "64% functional correctness" figure is not clearly traceable to a specific row in Table 2 as shown in the visible pages (the closest match, Level 2 Pass@10 ER, is 40.48%, and Pass@100 mean ER is 88.89%), which raises a question about exactly which condition the headline number represents; this should be checked against the full paper before quoting it. - No explicit statement of train/benchmark decontamination between Ascend-CoT's real-world kernel sources and NPUKernelBench's 158 tasks. - Level 3 results (the hardest, most practically relevant kernels — Gemm, TopK, attention-related) are reported but the paper does not analyze why RL fails to move the needle there beyond the general error taxonomy (Figure 6), leaving the central open problem for future work under-examined given it is the tier that matters most for real deployment. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or evaluating LLM-based kernel generation for non-CUDA accelerators gains a concrete, hardware-validated training recipe and a diagnostic benchmark, but should treat the reported gains as measured against a weak zero-shot baseline rather than against the current state of competing kernel-generation methods, and should note that the hardest kernel tier remains largely unsolved.
Recommended
#31 — Innovative (60)

Why Can't I Open My Drawer? Mitigating Object-Driven Shortcuts in Zero-Shot Compositional Action Recognition (39 ▲ )

Geo Ahn, Inwoong Lee, Taeoh Kim, Minho Shim, Dongyoon Wee, Jinwoo Choi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 10 Jul 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RCORE, a training framework combining co-occurrence-aware supervision and a temporal-order regularizer that reduces object-driven shortcut learning in zero-shot compositional action recognition (ZS-CAR). - Why it's important: Video models that recognize verb-object compositions by keying on the object rather than the observed motion fail to generalize to unseen verb-object pairs, undermining the entire premise of compositional generalization in action recognition. - How it does it: Co-occurrence Prior Regularization (CPR) synthesizes new compositions by pasting motion-region pixels from a donor video onto a target's high-motion mask and margin-penalizes frequent seen pairs as hard negatives, while Temporal Order Regularization for Composition (TORC) pushes verb features away from their temporally reversed and shuffled counterparts via a cosine-similarity loss plus an entropy-maximization term. - The good: Two new diagnostic metrics (False Seen Prediction and False Co-occurrence Prediction) plus a Compositional Gap statistic give quantitative, reproducible evidence that the SOTA baseline (C2C) collapses toward seen/frequent pairs (FSP rising 53%→63%, FCP 26%→29% over training) and that RCORE reverses this trend (47%→44%, 23%→23%). - The missing: All results come from a single research group's re-implementation of baselines (C2C's actual released weights are used, but Jung et al.'s CLIP variant is re-implemented since code is unavailable), and no significance tests, multiple seeds, or confidence intervals are reported for any accuracy number in Tables 2-5. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building compositional or zero-shot video-action systems, especially egocentric-vision practitioners, because the FSP/FCP diagnostic pair is a reusable tool beyond this paper's specific fix; general video-language researchers can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (58) is the main drag: no error bars, seeds, or significance tests appear anywhere, yet many reported deltas are under 2 points, and one baseline (Jung et al.'s CLIP variant) is a self re-implementation. Novelty (62) is moderate because the individual components are recombinations of known techniques (temporal-shuffle regularization adapted from Yun et al. ICML 2022; margin-based hard-negative losses are standard); the reusable contributions are the FSP/FCP/Delta-CG diagnostic triple and the EK100-com benchmark, not RCORE itself. Completeness (65) is helped by the honest report that CPR alone produces a seen-unseen trade-off (-4.2 seen, +3.1 unseen), i.e. no free lunch. Cross-backbone and cross-dataset replication of both the diagnosis and the fix is the strongest evidence here. Weighted composite: 0.20*62 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*60 = 60.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Zero-shot compositional action recognition requires a model trained on a subset of verb-object pairs to correctly classify held-out (unseen) pairs at test time, given that the verb and object vocabularies themselves are fixed and shared across splits. This matters for robotics and video understanding systems that must recognize novel combinations of known actions and objects (e.g., "closing" applied to an object only ever seen with "opening" in training) without retraining. Prior ZS-CAR methods condition verb prediction on object features via factorized logits, but the paper argues this conditioning creates a failure mode: because compositional training data is sparse (only 12.8% of the verb-object space is covered in the Sth-com benchmark) and skewed toward a handful of frequent pairs, and because objects are recognizable from a single frame while verbs require multi-frame temporal reasoning, models learn to predict verbs from the co-occurring object rather than from motion evidence. Key Idea: Object-driven shortcuts in ZS-CAR stem from two compounding, quantifiable factors, sparse/skewed co-occurrence statistics in training data and an asymmetric learning-difficulty gap between objects (single-frame, easy) and verbs (multi-frame, hard), and both can be independently targeted: co-occurrence bias by injecting synthetic supervision for absent compositions with a hard-negative margin penalty, and the difficulty gap by an explicit temporal-order-sensitivity loss on verb features. This is a diagnosis-then-fix contribution rather than a single novel mechanism; each individual regularizer (CPR, TORC) draws on established techniques (mixup-like frame compositing, contrastive margin losses, temporal-shuffle regularization) recombined for this specific failure mode. Methodology: The pipeline extracts frame-level features F ∈ R^(T×D) from a backbone (CLIP-B/16 or InternVideo2-CLIP-B/14, adapter-tuned via AIM or LoRA respectively), then splits into verb features F^V and object features f^O via dedicated encoders, with text embeddings E^V, E^O from class-name prompts. CPR synthesizes a new training video by alpha-blending (λ ∈ [0,1]) the high-motion-masked region of a donor video's object into a source video's motion region (Eq. 3), assigns soft labels for the resulting composition (Eqs. 4-5), and applies a margin loss (Eq. 7, margin m) that forces the synthesized composition's logit to outrank frequent seen "hard negative" compositions sharing either the verb or the object; a batch-adaptive label-space expansion (union of seen labels and newly synthesized labels, not the full V×O space) keeps cross-entropy training stable. TORC forms reversed (F_rev) and randomly shuffled (F_shuffled) versions of the frame sequence, then (i) minimizes cosine similarity between the original and reversed verb feature vectors (L_cos) to force order-sensitivity, and (ii) maximizes prediction entropy over verb classes when features are temporally shuffled (L_ent, Eq. 8) so the model cannot confidently guess the verb from scrambled temporal cues. Total loss is a weighted sum L_total = αL_com + βL_comp + γL_TORC + δL_CPR (four hyperparameters, values given in the appendix per the text but not shown in the extracted pages). Two of the three design decisions are reasonably justified empirically (the motion-mask-based synthesis targets exactly the object-driven-shortcut failure mode diagnosed earlier in the paper; the hard-negative restriction to frequent seen pairs, versus penalizing all hard negatives, is validated by ablation in Table 5(d), improving H.M. from ~31 to 37.7); the choice of λ and mask estimator for synthesis, and the four loss weights, are not justified beyond "following prior work" citations for the adapter-tuning method, leaving open whether performance is sensitive to these choices. Key Results: On Sth-com (79K videos, 161 verbs, 248 objects) under the paper's open-world unbiased protocol, RCORE improves verb@unseen-comp by 4.6 points and unseen composition accuracy by 3.8 points over C2C with a CLIP backbone (30.08%→33.90% Δ_CG-adjusted, and 25.25%→... per Table 2 the unseen composition accuracy moves 30.08→33.90 with CLIP, 39.53→43.98 with InternVideo2), and RCORE's Δ_CG on unseen compositions turns positive (+0.66 CLIP, +0.95 InternVideo2) versus negative for all baselines. On the newly introduced EK100-com (71K egocentric videos from EPIC-KITCHENS-100, 81 verbs, 216 objects, only 7.5% label coverage versus Sth-com's 12.8%), RCORE improves unseen composition accuracy by 6.9 points (CLIP) and 7.0 points (InternVideo2) over C2C, again with positive Δ_CG (+1.32, +3.83). Baselines compared are C2C (current SOTA, ECCV 2024, code available), Jung et al. (ICCV 2025, CLIP variant re-implemented since code unavailable for CLIP), and independent-modeling baselines AIM/LoRA; these are current and reasonably strong. Under the closed-world test-tuned-bias protocol used by prior work, RCORE achieves the best H.M. (43.2 vs. 42.8/42.6) and AUC (25.3 vs. 23.8/23.5) among CLIP-backbone methods, and best among enhanced variants (46.1 H.M., 27.5 AUC vs. C2C-enhance's 42.6/23.5). Testing scale is standard-benchmark scale, not stress-scale; no experiments at larger vocabulary sizes or dataset scales are reported. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD (RCORE: CPR + TORC combination) and NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (EK100-com). The diagnostic metrics (FSP, FCP, Δ_CG) are a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING/measurement framework applied to an existing problem. Genuinely novel: the motion-mask-based video-compositing synthesis strategy for generating absent verb-object pairs, and the FSP/FCP diagnostic pair for quantifying shortcut collapse in compositional recognition. Not novel: temporal reversal/shuffle regularization for verb representations is a direct adaptation of prior temporal-order-sensitivity work (cited as [46], Yun et al. ICML 2022) to this setting; margin-based hard-negative losses are standard. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and reasonably thorough: Table 5 isolates CPR-alone, TORC-alone, CPR+TORC, the L_cos/L_ent split within TORC, three label-space strategies, and three penalty-set choices in CPR, each showing consistent directional improvements that support the paper's causal story. However, no error bars, multiple random seeds, or significance tests appear anywhere in the extracted tables; all reported numbers are single-run point estimates, which is a meaningful gap given some deltas are under 2 points (e.g., EK100-com InternVideo2 seen accuracy differences). Code is released at github.com/KHU-VLL/RCORE (URL stated in abstract). Evaluation is done under the paper's own newly proposed "open-world unbiased" protocol as primary, with closed-world reported for comparison, which is methodologically sound (it avoids the acknowledged test-set-tuned bias-calibration problem in prior work) but means direct comparison to some literature numbers requires the paper's own re-evaluation of baselines, introducing re-implementation risk (explicitly flagged for Jung et al.'s CLIP variant). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (all claims are experimentally measured on two video benchmarks with two backbones). - Practical: Runs on standard adapter-tuned VLM backbones (CLIP-B/16, InternVideo2-CLIP-B/14) with AIM/LoRA fine-tuning, consistent with commercially available GPU hardware; no unusual compute infrastructure claimed. - Efficient: Not stated. No wall-clock training time, GPU-hour, or parameter-count comparison between RCORE and baselines is given in the extracted content; CPR's video-synthesis step (per-frame motion masking via a learning-free estimator) adds compute overhead not quantified. - Effective: Evidence directly measures the stated problem (unseen-composition accuracy and the FSP/FCP shortcut diagnostics) rather than only a proxy, and improvements are consistent across two backbones and two datasets, which strengthens the claim. - Scalable: Not tested. Both benchmarks are in the tens-of-thousands-of-videos, hundreds-of-classes range; no experiment probes behavior at 10x-100x vocabulary size or dataset scale, and the batch-adaptive label-space expansion (chosen specifically because full V×O cross-entropy "severely hurts" performance, per Table 5(c)) suggests the method may not trivially scale to much larger compositional spaces without further tuning. The paper delivers a released code repository (github.com/KHU-VLL/RCORE) and a new benchmark dataset (EK100-com), stopping short of a trained-model release claim in the extracted text. Strengths: - The FSP/FCP/Δ_CG diagnostic framework is a genuine methodological contribution independent of RCORE itself: it operationalizes "shortcut reliance" as a measurable training-time quantity rather than an anecdotal observation, and the controlled bias-aligned/bias-conflict experiment (Figure 2b) isolating co-occurrence-driven shortcuts from pretraining effects is a clean causal-diagnosis design. - The open-world evaluation protocol change (no test-set-tuned bias calibration, inference spans the full V×O space) directly addresses a specific, named flaw in prior closed-world ZS-CAR evaluation, and the paper reports both protocols so readers can compare. - Cross-backbone, cross-dataset consistency: the object-driven-shortcut diagnosis and RCORE's fix both replicate on two backbones (CLIP, InternVideo2) and two datasets (Sth-com, the new EK100-com), reducing the risk that findings are backbone- or dataset-specific artifacts. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper explicitly notes that Jung et al.'s method degrades under EK100-com's higher compositional sparsity (composition accuracy drops 3.2 points seen, 4.6 points unseen versus C2C), suggesting their own diagnostic framework generalizes to critiquing other methods' limits, not just the baseline they improve on. The paper acknowledges CPR alone produces a "seen-unseen trade-off" (reduces seen accuracy by 4.2 points while improving unseen by 3.1), i.e., it does not claim a free lunch from co-occurrence regularization alone. No explicit statement of compute cost, failure cases of RCORE itself, or scale limits is present in the extracted sections. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No error bars or repeated-seed variance are reported anywhere, so the practical significance of sub-2-point gains (common throughout Tables 2-4) versus baselines cannot be assessed from the paper alone. - The CPR synthesis mechanism (pasting a donor object's motion region into a target video, Eq. 3) is itself a fairly strong assumption that motion masks reliably isolate the action-relevant region across arbitrary donor-target pairs; the paper doesn't report failure-mode analysis of the synthesis step itself (e.g., how often the learning-free motion estimator produces degenerate or object-inconsistent masks). - The training/compute overhead of generating synthetic compositions per batch (motion-mask extraction, blending) relative to the baseline is not quantified, which matters for an "efficient" deployability judgment. - Hyperparameter sensitivity (λ blending strength, margin m, the four loss weights α/β/γ/δ) is not explored via sweep or sensitivity analysis in the extracted pages, so it is unclear how brittle RCORE's gains are to these choices. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on compositional or egocentric video-action recognition gains a reusable diagnostic toolkit (FSP/FCP/Δ_CG) and a validated, if incrementally novel, two-part regularizer that consistently improves unseen-composition accuracy by 3-7 points across two backbones and two datasets under a more rigorous open-world protocol than prior ZS-CAR work.
Recommended 2601.16211 PDF
#32 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Evaluating Direct Preference Optimization for Personalizing German Automatic Text Simplifications for Persons with Intellectual Disabilities

Y Gao, K Johnson, D Fröhlich, L Carrer, S Ebling · aclanthology.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HF4ATS, the largest German-language human preference dataset for automatic text simplification (ATS), and uses it to evaluate whether direct preference optimization (DPO) can personalize LLM-based ATS models to the preferences of persons with intellectual disabilities. - Why it's important: Persons with intellectual disabilities are a target population for accessible text but are rarely consulted during AI system design, and preference-based personalization is one of the few HITL methods compatible with legal restrictions on building identifiable user profiles for this group. - How it does it: SFT on filtered DEPlain complex-simple pairs, followed by group-specific DPO post-training on pairwise preferences collected from 15 target-group and 4 expert annotators via a tablet-based annotation tool, with effectiveness measured through win rate, supremacy scores, and annotator agreement. - The good: The controlled comparison across six preference-data subsets (all, equal-information, model-matched, max-Intra-AA, max-Inter-AA) isolates preference consistency, not model backbone or data heuristics, as the dominant driver of DPO gains (up to +31.93% win rate for the max-Inter-AA subset). - The missing: The negative result for the target group (DPO underperforms SFT for most target annotators, Table 5) is only explained post hoc via low Intra-AA/Inter-AA scores; the paper does not test whether simpler interventions (larger annotator pool, longitudinal sessions, alternative elicitation formats) would raise consistency enough to make DPO work for this group. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ATS researchers and accessible-AI practitioners who care about participatory design methodology and preference-learning failure modes with vulnerable annotator populations; the core DPO mechanics are standard, so readers only interested in the algorithm can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Completeness (78) is high: the paper reports and analyzes a negative result (DPO underperforms SFT for 11 of 12 target-annotator/model pairs), recommends an alternative (KTO), and lists four limitations without minimizing them. Novelty (60) and Non-Obviousness (55) are the limiters. The DPO objective is used unmodified from Rafailov et al. 2023; the contribution is the HF4ATS dataset plus the consistency-gates-DPO finding, not any method. Rigor (62) is undercut by a circularity the paper does not resolve: the 4 target and 4 expert annotators used for final human evaluation are selected for highest Intra-AA, which is the very variable the paper claims drives DPO success, and Intra-AA is NA for several target annotators. Impact (45) is low because the paper delivers no working personalization method for its stated beneficiaries. Weighted composite: 0.20*60 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*45 = 59.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 78 Resilience 55 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: German automatic text simplification systems built on LLMs are typically SFT-only and not adapted to the specific comprehension needs of persons with intellectual disabilities, a population with well-documented struggles with complex sentence structure, jargon, and implicit metaphor. Because building identifiable user profiles for this group is ethically and, in many jurisdictions, legally restricted, individualized personalization (e.g., via prompting on stored preferences) is not viable; the paper targets group-level personalization using only preference feedback, without user profiles, as the accessible alternative. Prior ATS research involving this population has mostly confined their input to late-stage evaluation rather than iterative preference collection that feeds back into model training. Key Idea: Apply the validate-annotate-evaluate human-in-the-loop principle to DPO post-training itself, treating annotator preference consistency (measured via Intra- and Inter-annotator agreement) as the variable that determines whether offline preference optimization succeeds, rather than treating DPO as a black-box alignment step. The paper's central empirical claim is that DPO's effectiveness for personalization is gated by preference-signal quality, not by which model backbone or which SFT checkpoint generated the candidate pairs. Methodology: Pipeline: (1) SFT phase — DEPlain-APA complex-simple pairs filtered by a four-step pipeline (many-to-many mapping removal, Sentence-BERT cosine-similarity threshold of 0.5, ROUGE-1/2/L F1 > 0.8 near-duplicate removal, >30-word simplification removal), reducing 13,122 pairs to a 5,200-pair stratified split (3,600 train/800 dev/800 test); four backbones (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, DiscoLeo-Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct, LeoLM-Mistral-7B-Chat) fine-tuned with LoRA (rank 16) under AdamW, checkpoint-selected via SARI and WSTF4 on the dev set. (2) DPO pair creation — 20 inferences per SFT checkpoint per complex sentence (8 prompt templates x 4 decoding configs), reviewed by 13 trained German-speaking pair creators into preference pairs subject to entailment and equal-information rubrics, yielding HF4ATS-DPO (6,018 pairs, 3,009 unique). (3) DPO post-training — standard Bradley-Terry/DPO objective (Rafailov et al. 2023) with beta=0.1, frozen SFT reference model, trained separately per annotator group (target vs. expert) and per six preference-data subsets (all, all_=, LLM_=, max Intra-AA, max Inter-AA) to isolate factors. (4) Evaluation — automatic metrics (SARI, BERTScore, WSTF4, win rate via implicit reward margin) plus human evaluation via a DPO "supremacy score" (fraction of pairwise A/B choices favoring DPO over SFT output for the same input), computed per-annotator-group and tested for significance with one-sided binomial tests (scipy). Design decisions: using implicit-reward DPO instead of explicit reward modeling was well-justified given HITL cognitive burden and no explicit numeric preference elicitation from the target group; the choice of group-level (not individual-level) personalization is justified on legal/ethical grounds but forecloses any test of finer-grained personalization; the decision to run six independent DPO trainings per subset is well-suited to the ablation goal but multiplies noise given each expert/target group already has few annotators (4 each in final evaluation). Key Results: Automatic evaluation (Table 3): DPO improves WSTF4 readability over SFT for all backbones (e.g., DiscoLeo SFT WSTF4 6.515 -> DPO-expert 4.031) but BERTScore drops (0.9049 -> 0.7814), indicating semantic drift; SARI is mixed, largely preserved or improved for expert-supervised models, declining for target-supervised ones. Win rates against DPO test set are consistently higher for expert-trained checkpoints (0.6118-0.6118) than target-trained (0.4382-0.5211), with two of three target models falling below the 0.50 win-rate threshold entirely. Subset ablation (Table 4): training on maximum-agreement subsets yields the largest gains, up to +31.93% win rate for LeoLM-Mistral target-group DPO on the max Intra-AA subset; information-equality and model-matched subsets often degrade performance. Human evaluation (Table 5, supremacy scores): expert annotators favor DPO over SFT in 3/4 cases per backbone, with one result reaching significance (DiscoLeo-Llama expert, supremacy 0.74, p<0.05, double-asterisked as significant even excluding an outlier); target-group annotators favor SFT over DPO in most cases (only 1/12 annotator-model combinations favors DPO), and no target result reaches significance. All experiments run on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU with 8B-parameter backbones — a modest, standard-scale test, not a stress test. Annotator agreement (Table 1) is low to moderate for both groups (target Cohen's kappa mostly 0.00-0.30, several negative/near-zero; expert Krippendorff's alpha 0.42-0.76), underscoring the noisy-preference-signal explanation for the negative target-group result. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (HF4ATS, the first and largest German ATS human preference dataset collected directly from persons with intellectual disabilities) and NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (preference consistency, not model choice, gates DPO personalization success; DPO personalization fails for the target group specifically). The DPO training procedure itself is a standard application of existing DPO (Rafailov et al. 2023) to a new setting; no new formulation, method, or system is proposed. The genuinely novel contribution is the dataset plus the negative empirical result and its consistency-based explanation, not the algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and are the paper's main analytical contribution (six preference-data subsets x three backbones x two annotator groups = systematic factor isolation). Standard deviations are reported for automatic metrics (Table 3); significance testing (one-sided binomial, scipy, p<0.05) is applied to the human supremacy scores, a stronger standard than most ATS personalization papers. However: the final human evaluation set is only 300 ATS pairs (50 per winning checkpoint) drawn from only 4 target and 4 expert annotators (selected as the highest-agreement annotators, a selection that likely inflates apparent consistency benefits relative to the full 15-target-participant pool); Intra-AA is unavailable (NA) for several target annotators due to missing repeat pairs, weakening the consistency-measurement backbone of the paper's central claim. Dataset and code are stated as released (icons in abstract) but no URL is given in the excerpted text. Ethics approval was obtained from the University of Zurich ethics committee for target-group data collection, a genuine strength for work involving a vulnerable population. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (LLM-based ATS system evaluated by experiment). Practical: Yes — 8B-parameter backbones with LoRA fine-tuning run on a single A100 GPU, commercially accessible hardware. Efficient: Not quantified; no inference latency, throughput, or memory numbers are reported, only training compute (single GPU, LoRA rank 16). Effective: Partially — DPO measurably improves readability (WSTF4) and modestly improves SARI for expert-supervised models, but BERTScore declines indicate the improvement trades off semantic fidelity, and the target-group result shows DPO does not solve the stated personalization problem for its primary intended beneficiaries. Scalable: Not tested — the dataset (6,018 preference pairs from 19 total annotators) is small relative to typical RLHF-scale preference sets (tens of thousands to millions of pairs), and the paper explicitly flags target-group data collection at greater scale as impractical due to cognitive burden; no experiment at 10x-100x scale is attempted or claimed. The paper delivers open-source models and a dataset (per abstract icons) but the annotation web tool and pair-creation scripts are described but not benchmarked for reuse cost. Strengths: - The negative result for the target group is reported and analyzed rather than suppressed; the paper explicitly concludes DPO may be ill-suited for target-group ATS personalization and recommends alternatives (KTO), a genuine act of intellectual honesty uncommon in applied-DPO papers. - The preference-consistency-as-mediator analysis (Table 4 subset ablation, cross-referenced against Table 1 agreement scores) is a real methodological contribution: it isolates why DPO succeeds or fails, not just whether it does, which is more useful to downstream practitioners than a single win/loss number. - The inclusive annotation workflow (caretaker-mediated sessions, simplified-language consent, tablet UI, repeated pairs for agreement measurement) is carefully engineered for a population with intellectual disabilities and is documented in enough detail (Appendix A, Figure 5) to be reused by other accessibility-AI projects. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly: (1) the number of target-group annotators remains limited and preference variability within the group may not be fully captured; (2) the study addresses group-level, not individual-level, personalization, and stronger effects may emerge with finer-grained or longitudinal preference modeling; (3) DPO was evaluated as a representative offline method and findings may not generalize to online or alternative alignment frameworks; (4) the cognitive burden of pairwise annotation constrained data scale, which may have limited DPO's effectiveness, and lower-burden feedback paradigms are left to future work. This is a direct, non-minimizing confrontation of the study's central weakness. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The final human-evaluation annotator subset (4 target, 4 expert) is selected for highest Intra-AA, which is the same variable the paper claims drives DPO success; this creates a partial circularity where the reported target-group failure may be a floor rather than a ceiling estimate of what higher-consistency subsets could achieve, and the paper does not test whether the excluded, lower-agreement target annotators would have failed even more severely (or, symmetrically, whether the full target pool might show more promise under different consistency-boosting interventions). - Two of four expert annotators (ea01, ea03) could not see the complex source text during annotation to match target-group conditions, while the other two could; this creates a within-expert-group condition split that is acknowledged but not separately analyzed for whether it affects the reported win rates or supremacy scores. - No comparison against a non-DPO personalization baseline (e.g., personalized prompting, in-context few-shot from group preferences, as mentioned in Related Work) is run; the paper compares DPO-vs-SFT only, so the claim that DPO specifically (rather than any post-training signal) is what's driving expert-group gains is not isolated from simpler alternatives. - BERTScore decline under DPO (0.90 -> 0.78-0.84) is described as "semantic drift" but never manually inspected or reported with example outputs in the main text; a reviewer would want qualitative failure cases to distinguish benign paraphrase variation from actual meaning loss, especially given the target population's vulnerability to unfaithful simplifications. Bottom Line: The paper is a useful, honestly-reported case study on the limits of offline preference optimization when consulting a vulnerable, low-consistency annotator population, and its dataset and workflow have reuse value for accessible-AI researchers, but the underlying DPO technique is unmodified from prior work and practitioners seeking a working personalization method for intellectual-disability-focused ATS will not find one delivered here.
Search Results
#33 — Moderately Innovative (58)

A Comprehensive Study of Implementation Bugs in Multi-modal Agents

S Li, L Bu, S Liu, Y Wang, G Bai, F Xie, K Chen, C Yue · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper builds a taxonomy of implementation bugs specific to multi-modal LLM agents (M-agents) from 158 bugs mined across 34 GitHub repositories, then implements MATester, a runtime tool that flags bugs by comparing Perceptor/Planner/Executor outputs against the environment and task. - Why it's important: M-agents are already deployed in safety-critical settings (autonomous driving, robotics, GUI automation) where crashes or misbehavior cause traffic accidents, financial loss, or privacy leaks, yet no prior study isolates bugs unique to multi-modal perception and cross-modal action. - How it does it: A top-down taxonomy splits bugs into 6 end-user symptoms, 16 component-level symptoms (Perceptor/Planner/Executor), and 7 root causes, derived by manually coding 412 bug reports down to 158 agent-specific bugs (Cohen's Kappa 0.84), then MATester runs a GPT-4o judging LLM over instrumented inter-component logs to detect these symptoms automatically. - The good: MATester, built purely from the paper's own taxonomy, recovers 61.4% of already-known open issues across 12 held-out agents and surfaces 31 previously unreported bugs, giving the taxonomy a validation beyond post-hoc labeling of the same data it was derived from. - The missing: The taxonomy and MATester both depend on manual instrumentation of each agent to emit snapshot/plan/action/reflect logs, and the paper does not report false-positive rates for MATester (i.e., how many detector-flagged symptoms are not real bugs) beyond a single-run 97.6% manual-validation accuracy figure on 41 flagged bugs. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building, testing, or debugging LLM agents that consume non-textual (image/audio/video) environment state, since it is the first systematic empirical catalogue of where these agents fail and why; less relevant to text-only agent researchers.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (an empirical bug study whose tool, MATester, is a proof-of-concept rather than the primary claim). Rigor (60) and Completeness (65) are the load-bearing dimensions: two independent coders with Cohen's Kappa 0.84 post-consolidation, 158 labeled bugs from 1,268 raw issues, and a public code/data release are real reproducibility positives, but MATester's precision rests on manual validation of just 41 flagged bugs, the 61.4% figure measures overlap with human-reported issues rather than ground truth, and no ablation tests whether majority-of-3 GPT-4o or temperature 0.2 actually drives the result. Novelty (60) reflects a genuinely first-of-kind M-agent bug taxonomy, though the methodology (open coding, Kappa, LLM-as-judge) is standard machinery applied to a new artifact class, which also holds Non-Obviousness to 45. Impact (60) credits 31 previously unreported bugs found across 12 held-out agents but discounts the unquantified per-agent manual instrumentation cost. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*60 = 58.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based agents that must perceive and act in dynamic, multi-modal environments (autonomous driving, robotics, GUI automation) face implementation failure modes that single-modal (text-only) agents do not: cross-modal perception errors, dynamic-object handling, and action/tool mismatches with heterogeneous environments. Prior empirical bug studies of LLM agents (e.g., Rahardja et al. on 201 general agent bugs, Cemri et al. on multi-agent failure modes) analyze bugs at the code or general-module level and do not isolate what breaks specifically because an agent must fuse and act on multi-modal, real-time input. Practitioners deploying M-agents in safety-critical domains currently have no systematic reference for what kinds of bugs to expect, how they manifest, or how to test for them. Key Idea: The paper's organizing insight is that M-agent bugs should be classified along the agent's own inter-component data flow (Perceptor snapshot to Planner plan to Executor action to environment), because a bug's root cause and observable symptom are best distinguished by which stage's output diverges from ground truth (the actual environment state or task). This three-tier symptom structure (global/end-user, component-level, root cause) is a reframing/organization of known agent architecture rather than a new algorithmic technique, and the paper is explicit that its practical contribution is the taxonomy plus a proof-of-concept checker, not a new bug-fixing method. Methodology: Three stages. (1) Data collection: 84 candidate M-agents gathered from GitHub, top-tier venues (ACL, CHI, CVPR, ICCV, NeurIPS, ICLR, ICSE, FSE, ISSTA, TPAMI, JMLR, TSE, TOSEM 2023-2025), and a prior survey, filtered via a quasi-gold-standard keyword+snowballing method, then fine-grained manual code inspection down to 46 agents with complete Perceptor/Planner/Executor implementations (34 for taxonomy construction, 12 held out for evaluation). (2) Bug labeling: 1,268 raw GitHub issues/PRs filtered by keyword search (263 candidates) plus manual review of the remainder, yielding 412 bug-related reports; two authors independently code global symptoms, component symptoms, and root causes on an 11% sample (initial Kappa 0.42, resolved via consensus meeting to 0.84 on the remaining data), producing 158 unique agent-specific bugs.
